coredata.dc:title: State-of-the-art nanosensors and kits for the detection of antibiotic residues in milk and dairy products

coredata.dc:description: Antibiotic resistance is increasingly seen as a future concern, but antibiotics are still commonly used in animals, leading to their accumulation in humans through the food chain and posing health risks. The development of nanomaterials has opened up possibilities for creating new sensing strategies to detect antibiotic residues, resulting in the emergence of innovative nanobiosensors with different benefits like rapidity, simplicity, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision. Therefore, this comprehensive review provides pertinent and current insights into nanomaterials-based electrochemical/optical sensors for the detection of antibitic residues (ANBr) across milk and dairy products. Here, we first discuss the commonly used ANBs in real products, the significance of ANBr, and also their binding/biological properties. Then, we provide an overview of the role of using different nanomaterials on the development of advanced nanobiosensors like fluorescence-based, colorimetric, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, surface plasmon resonance, and several important electrochemical nanobiosensors relying on different kinds of electrodes. The enhancement of ANB electrochemical behavior for detection is also outlined, along with a concise overview of the utilization of (bio)recognition units. Ultimately, this paper offers a perspective on the future concepts of this research field and commercialized nanomaterial-based sensors to help upgrade the sensing techniques for ANBr in dairy products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotic resistance is increasingly seen as a future concern, but antibiotics are still commonly used in animals, leading to their accumulation in humans through the food chain and posing health risks. The development of nanomaterials has opened up possibilities for creating new sensing strategies to detect antibiotic residues, resulting in the emergence of innovative nanobiosensors with different benefits like rapidity, simplicity, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision. Therefore, this comprehensive review provides pertinent and current insights into nanomaterials-based electrochemical/optical sensors for the detection of antibitic residues (ANBr) across milk and dairy products. Here, we first discuss the commonly used ANBs in real products, the significance of ANBr, and also their binding/biological properties. Then, we provide an overview of the role of using different nanomaterials on the development of advanced nanobiosensors like fluorescence-based, colorimetric, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, surface plasmon resonance, and several important electrochemical nanobiosensors relying on different kinds of electrodes. The enhancement of ANB electrochemical behavior for detection is also outlined, along with a concise overview of the utilization of (bio)recognition units. Ultimately, this paper offers a perspective on the future concepts of this research field and commercialized nanomaterial-based sensors to help upgrade the sensing techniques for ANBr in dairy products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety and security are fundamental issues in today's world [1]. Therefore, it seems absolutely mandatory to control and trace all types of hazards (chemical, physical and biological), contaminants, and residues that have the possibility of entering the food chain [2]. Antibiotics (ANBs) are known as one of the persistent organic pollutants (POPs), leading to expansion of resistant pathogens, failure to respond to ANB doses, allergic reactions, teratogenicity, and cancer risks [3]. ANBs such as aminoglycosides, sulfonamides, beta-lactams, quinolones, tetracyclines (TTC), macrolides, and chloramphenicol (CAP) are used arbitrarily and unauthorized for therapeutic/ medicine purposes, feed supplements, increasing the growth and productivity of livestock, and improving the quality of feed in large quantities (Fig. 1 ) [3,4]. They could also be used for animal treatment in case of diseases. However, even the correct use of ANBs could lead to ANB residues (ANBr) in dairy products, which threaten the health of consumers as a risk and concern. For this reason, the use of ANBs is forbidden in many countries, especially in the European Union. This issue is linked to the principle of maximum residue limit (MRL) which is discussed in more detail in the following.

ANBs are one of the common contaminating agents of dairy products, which in low levels lead to the destruction of starter bacteria or prevent their growth in rennet, yogurt, or fermented products and create economical, technological, and sanitary problems in the dairy industry [5–7]. Accordingly, ANBr prevent the synthesis of nucleic acids and lead to mutations in the production genes of some enzymes, disturbance in the energy production system and finally, the synthesis of proteins [8]. Antimicrobial resistance is also one of the most frequent negative effects of ANBrs in food products and has grown to be a significant global issue [9]. As such, through the food chain, humans can become infected with resistant bacterial diseases, rendering ANB therapy ineffective [10]. Considering the adverse effects of ANBs, continuous monitoring and tracing of ANBr through organizations seems mandatory. Another critical point is to reduce the threshold concentration of these compounds, which even in low levels have a detrimental effect on human health because new thermal processes on dairy products such as pasteurization are not adequate to mitigate or eliminate ANBs from the dairy products [7]. Therefore, rapid detection tests and strict monitoring of ANBr are considered a major duty for the dairy industry and farmers to assure the safety and quality of final products [11].

European Food Safety Legislation Institute has evaluated antibacterial compounds in the livestock farming since 1950s [12]. These compounds have a different function compared to other antimicrobial agents for animals including preventative agents, treatmenting effects, feed fortification and growth enhancement [13]. Access to systematic, and trustworthy information about ANBr is not simple, particularly in developing countries. Considering the information published by the European Federation of Animal Health [14], in 1999 almost 3900 tons of ANBs were used in the European Union (EU) in veterinary medicine and 800 tons as growth promoters. Kools et al. [15] stated the most used ANBs are TTC, beta-lactams, and sulfonamides. There are some challenges about consumption of ANBs in animal feed and their effects in peoples. The main important problem is possibility of the resistance to ANBs due to consumption of animal-based products [16]. As regard to noncompliant results from the EU report about ANBr to support the people from their risks, the sanitary authorities and different regulatory agencies have launched legislative actions containing the establishment of MRL and the development of regulate programs on food produced in the market and countries [17,18]. The absorption of ANBs in animals after administration is weak and mostly excreted (70–90%) [19]. However, it has been noted that this is not true for all ANBs. It should be noted that these ANBr remain unchanged in the environment. Because, if all ANBs are mainly excreted, the dairy industry wouldn't have a problem with ANBr in animal bodies [20,21]. The ANBr have polluting impacts on the environment (water and soil) as well as agricultural crops. For instance, in the soil, ANBr are responsible for anoxic denitrification and consequently affect bacterial communities responsible for this process [22].

Most of the assays, like chromatography and spectro-photometry methods, which are routinely performed for detecting ANBr in central labs and research centers, require expensive equipment, trained personnel, and are time-consuming. So basically, there's a need for a point-of-care device that's dependable, fast, and easy to use in order to meet or even improve the sensitivity standards of traditional techniques [23,24]. An alternative option is to use quick, accessible, and reliable immunological and microbiological tests [25,26]. There are various tests available in the market, such as the SNAP® tests (Maine, USA) [27], the Delvotest® (Indiana, USA) [26], the Eco test® BT (Cremona, Italy) [28], the LacTek® test (Maine, USA) [29], the BetaStar® Plus (Kentucky, USA) [30], etc.; which have a wide range of uses. These commercial tests are simple, cost-effective, portable, and regulatory approved that come in user-friendly kits with clear instructions [25].

However, as milk is a complicated sample, so false-positive results are also common. Factors like heating, natural inhibitors, colony-formed units, and various natural interferences can affect test results and make the analysis less reliable [25,31,32]. Hence, these tests may be limited in their ability to detect analytes accurately, impacting sensitivity, specificity, and the range of target analytes they can identify. Consequently, the utilization of a user-friendly, reliable, rapid response-time, sensitive, selective, and simply handled point-of-care platform that can amend the criteria of conventional methods with faster responses, has been demanded. Nowadays, the use of bio−/nano-sensors to detect ANBr has progressed owing to their unique characteristics including being user-friendly, great and ideal accuracy, high reproducibility/sensitivity/selectivity/stability, quick and short response time, easy experimental operation, and lower limit of detection (LOD) [33].

The major challenges in the development of biosensors are: (i) transformation of recognition signal into a specific signal (optical, electrochemical, electrical, gravimetric, or acoustic); (ii) amplifying transducer operation such as improvement of reproducibility, selectivity and sensitivity, and lower LOD, and (iii) use of micro/nano-preparation technologies to miniaturize the sensing tools. These challenges can be solved by using the combination of nanomaterials (NMs)-based sensors owing to color/ or structure tunability, possessing a large stability and surface area, shock-enduring abilities, and ideal conductivities. Nano/biomaterials are used mostly as signal amplification approaches in the development of nano/bio-sensors include nanoparticles (NPs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), dendrimers, enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, polymeric NPs, metal organic frameworks (MOFs), nanowires (NWs), nano-rods (NRs), and quantum dots (QDs), as depicted in [34].

Consequently, this comprehensive study discusses the performance of NMs-based sensors and kits to detect ANBr in dairy products. Owing to the complexity of the food matrix, interference in detection by these systems may occur. Investigating these topics in NMs-based sensors and kits based on different methods can result in better understanding of the application approaches and their challenges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiotics residues in milk and dairy products; their binding/biological properties
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned, unauthorized and arbitrary use of ANBs such as aminoglycosides, sulfonamides, β-lactams, quinolones, TTC, macrolides, and CAP in agro-food, and veterinary sectors and ANBr in the food chain and environment is leading to adverse environmental, economic and health complications [35]. Therefore, various regulatory agencies establish strict rules in different food products. In this regard, regulatory agencies have prohibited several particular antimicrobial growth promoters, while for those that are not still prohibited, MRLs have been estabilished (Table 1 ) to ensure health protection of consumers from exposure to ANBrs in animal-based dairy products. The MRL for allowable pharmaceuticals in food is usually established by estimating the amount of parent pharmaceuticals and metabolites allowable in food products obtained from treated animals. There is no risk to the consumer's health below this threshold. However, the amount above the determined MRL can pose significant threats to human health. In this manner, several screening approaches for sensitive and selective monitoring of veterinary drug residues are strongly needed to ensure that ANBr in animal foods are below the MRL [10].

In this regard, accurate, rapid, and ultra-sensitive detection and quantification of ANBr in dairy products is essential for ensuring consumer safety, preventing the development of ANB resistance, and avoiding allergic reactions [36]. Conventional screening methods for ANBr in food, rely on different techniques to detect and quantify the presence of ANBs. These methods include microbiological assays, immunoassays, and chromatographic techniques e.g., high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography (GC) [37]. These methods are routinely employed due to their simplicity, and high-throughput capabilities. For example, ELISA utilizes antibodies to specifically bind to ANBr and produce a measurable signal. Microbial inhibition tests involve the observation of bacterial growth inhibition in the presence of ANBr [38].

Conventional screening methods have proven effective in routine testing, but they do have limitations. One major drawback is their inability to detect a wide range of ANBs or their metabolites [39]. These methods are often limited to specific classes of ANBs and may not be sensitive enough to detect low levels of ANBr. This poses a significant challenge given the multitude of ANBs used in veterinary medicine and agriculture. Furthermore, there are other restrictions of conventional methods that hinder their widespread use. Regarding the physico-chemical methods, several issues are important, including the high consumption of chemical solvents, the requirement for expensive and specialized equipment, an inability to detect new compounds, limited sensitivity and specificity, and the complexity of food matrices. On the other hand, the microbiological and immunological methods are also expensive and time-consuming processes. Additionally, lengthy sample preparation and derivatization further add to the complexity of these methods [39,40].

Therefore, there is a need for alternative screening techniques that can overcome these limitations. Novel approaches should be developed to address the issues of detection range, sensitivity, and specificity. Moreover, reducing the use of chemical solvents and simplifying the sample preparation process would greatly enhance the feasibility and practicality of these methods [36,41]. One potential solution lies in the advancement of analytical technologies. For instance, the integration of HPLC with mass spectrometry (MS) allows for the simultaneous analysis of multiple ANBs and their metabolites [42]. This combination provides greater sensitivity and specificity compared to conventional methods. Additionally, the development of rapid screening methods, e.g., immunoassays or biosensors, could significantly reduce the time required for analysis. These techniques utilize specific ANBs or biological recognition elements to detect target compounds, offering a faster and more efficient screening process [37]. Furthermore, the utilization of microfluidic systems can simplify the sample preparation and analysis procedures. These portable systems have the potential to reduce the consumption of chemical solvents and the need for specialized equipment, making them more accessible and cost-effective [43].

Likewise, new methods utilize advanced technologies such as nano/biosensors. On the other hand, nano/biosensors utilize nanomaterials/ or biological components such as enzymes or ANBs to detect ANBr, offering high sensitivity, selevtivity and specificity, rapid screening and response, cost-effective and broad-spectrum detection [40]. One of the advantages of these new methods is their ability to detect a broader range of ANBs, including those not covered by conventional screening methods [36]. Moreover, new detection methods have shown improved sensitivity, allowing for the detection of ANBr at lower concentrations. This is important because even low levels of ANBr can have adverse effects on human health [44]. By employing advanced technologies, these methods can provide more accurate and reliable results, ensuring the safety of the food supply. However, it is important to note that the adoption of new detection methods also comes with challenges. Accordingly, the validation and standardization of these methods need to be established to ensure their reliability and comparability across different labs [45]. To conclude, while conventional screening methods have been effective in routine testing, they do have limitations that hinder their widespread use. By exploring novel approaches such as integrating HPLC with MS, developing rapid screening methods, and utilizing microfluidic systems, researchers can address these limitations and improve the efficiency, sensitivity, and practicality of ANBs screening in various industries.

One of the most effective parameters in improving the performance of the sensors is the affinity and proper junction of the target to the sensor components (NMs and biomolecules). ANBs should be efficiently conjugated on a carrier such as bio/nanomaterials, microfluidic chips, or electrodes to reach the sensing application in the food industry [35]. Nowadays, screening kits have been constructed for rapid ANBr detection in dairy products, based on binding to specific receptors in bioanalytical techniques, which provide responses in rapid times with a great selectivity and sensitivity [46].

ANBs contain a wide and unique range of chemical functional groups (e.g., -NH, -NH2, -COOH, -OH) around the central atom, which facilitate their interaction with other molecules. As a results, this characteristic can be used successfully to make ANB imprints within polymers [35]. Currently, many strategies have been focused on the application of biomolecules (DNA, RNA) and NMs hybrids. Two approaches are established to fabricate biomolecules-NMs hybrids; (i) covalent junction based on various kinds of chemical reactions (between alkynyl and azide groups), and the condensation reaction between carboxylic acid and amino groups, (ii) noncovalent junction along with hydrophobic and π-π stacking intermolecular interactions e.g. van der Waals forces, hydrogen and electrostatic bonding [47]. In molecular imprinted polymer (MIP)-based sensors, an antibody-like molecule is able to connect and distinguish between molecules [35]. Overall, in nano/biomaterial-based sensors, computational strategies, e.g. molecular docking, moleculars, quantum chemical calculations, machine learning, and molecular mechanics, are typically used to assess noncovalent interactions between molecules [35,46].

For example, Gutiérrez et al. [46] developed and applied a machine learning tool coupled with a nano−/bio-sensor to detect ANBr (kanamycin (KNM), ampicillin (AMP), oxy-TTC (OTC) and sulfadimethoxine) in raw milk. This nano−/bio-sensor was fabricated by chemical interaction between gold NPs (AuNPs) with aptamer bio-receptors for detection and analysis of broadly used ANBs. In milk, ANBr binded to aptamer and induced the aggregation of AuNPs. However, coupling the nano-biosensor with machine learning, led to the acceleration of detection and higher sensitivity and accuracy of the sensor. In the electrochemical-based sensors, behavior of targets is revealed by the side chains/functional groups and the major skeleton.

Regarding the restrictions of oxido-reduction behavior in ANBs, improving electrodes with NMs such as carbon NMs (CNMs) could be a successful approach to overcome this challenge. For instance, TTC possess electroactive functional groups in its structure which facilitates the reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-based electrochemical detection. When used as an electrode, TTC creates a positively charged structure by losing two electrons and a proton directly. Following, its combination with the strong negative oxygen in the hydron, leads to the development of a substance consisting of quinone [48]. Similarly, amoxicillin (AMX) detection was improved by using CNMs-modified electrode and graphene (G)/AuNPs owing to the redox behavior of the hydroxyl replacement with the corresponding carbonyl functional group on the molecule of AMX [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different nanomaterial-based sensors for detecting antibiotic residues in dairy products
ce:section.ce:para: In the design and fabrication of functional transducers for various bio−/nano-sensors or kits, as well as in sensing applications for detection of various of hazardous chemical substances in food products, NMs are thought to be the most attractive candidates because of their high electrical conductivity, unique magnetic attributes, high surface area, low-cost, high sensitivity, selectivity, good stability, portability and rapid response [51,52]. In general, NMs are also capable of both attaching bio-recognition elements as well as transducing signals. Different types of NMs (including CNMs, metal-oxides, noble metals or magnetic NPs, polymeric NPs, etc.) with unique physical/chemical/optical/ electronic properties have been recently used to design and construct many bio−/nano-sensor systems ( Fig. 2 ). The following sections provide a detailed description of different NMs-based sensors (e.g., electrochemical, optical, etc.,) for ANBr detection in milk and dairy products.

Electrochemical analysis has been found to be of significant interest for scientists because of its ease of use, rapid analysis, low instrumentation cost, in-situ analysis capability, and high sensitivity. The installation of biosensors on an active electrode is the fundamental step in electrochemical analysis. ANBs can be selectively recognized and captured by biosensing elements in analytical sensors; however, an electrochemical ANB detection mechanism relies on the electro-oxidation of ANBs [53]. Hence, the activity of the biosensor on the electrode surface has a significant impact on the selectivity and sensitivity of electrochemical measurement. ANBs can typically be bound by oligonucleotides or antibodies, or they can react with metal ions or other materials to produce electrochemical signals on electrodes that have been changed. In this regard, a quantitative study can be carried out since the electrical signal is proportionate to ANB concentration within a specific range. Furthermore, the use of modified electrodes is an established approach for determining ANB concentrations without direct electrochemical signals.

Carbonaceous compounds have drawn scientists' attention as an excellent functional electron transfer intermediate in view of some special characteristics like cost-effectiveness, small size, limited field emission, wide potential windows, environmental stability, chemical inertness, proper aspect ratio, high chemical and thermal stability, simple preparation, biocompatibility, and excellent electronic conductivity. The development of CNMs occupies a non-negligible position in the field of nano-science and nanotechnology, so that they have been able to greatly facilitate the rapid advancement of sensing technologies [54,55]. Substantial surface-to-volume ratio in CNMs is related to their unique geometrical properties, which ensures that more surface atoms are exposed and bind a greater number of sensing elements [56,57]. According to their spatial dimensions, inert nature, and regular nano-scale structure, CNMs are recently introduced as hopeful compounds creating novel opportunities in expanding of new-generation sensing systems [54,58,59].

CNTs, as a tubular 1D CNMs, are sp2 hybridized carbon units of rolled G sheets that were firstly introduced by Iijima in 1991 [60]. CNTs have recently received a lot of interest as one of the most effective CNMs for constructing nanosensors (NSs) and nano-electronics devices. CNTs are also effectively functionalized using a wide range of chemical groups that can bind organic or inorganic molecules. In this regard, functionalized CNTs have turned into an attractive candidate for the design and development of a variety of nano-devices [59,61]. Single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) and multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) are generally two different types of CNTs that can be characterized by the configuration of their G cylinders. These NMs can improve the sensitivity and functionalized properties of NSs, giving them a wider detection range with an ultralow LOD [24,58]. Some of the most common CNTs-based sensors assessed in the published works for the detection of ANBr are summarized in this section (Table 2).

Azadbakht and Abbasi [62] designed an impedimetric aptasensor for determination of KNM composed of CNTs together with molybdenum selenide nano-flowers (MoSe2) and AuNPs that served as a signal amplifier. AuNPs with a high surface area were incorporated to CNTs/MoSe2 for further modification and lead to easier aptamer immobilization. The authors confirmed that, amino group at the terminal of KNM-specific aptamer was covalently connected to the carboxyl agents of CNTs. As such, KNM was successfully determined in milk at LOD = 0.28 pM. They attributed the effective recognition capabilities of proposed sensor to various parameters, including porous structure and excellent adsorption ability of CNTs, high surface area and unique conductivity characteristics of MoSe2/AuNPs, as well as the strong affinity toward target analyte and covalent attachment bio-recognition element. Yousefi et al. [63] produced a sensitive electrochemical sensor based on MWCNTs/Pt-doped CdS NPs modified SPCE for the detection of natamycin in commercial yogurt drink and cheese samples. Using a voltammetry transduction technique, the proposed sensor over a linear dynamic range (LDR) of 0.2 to 70.0 μM provided a low LOD = 0.12 μM. The significant difference in the LOD of these two studies is closely attributed to the difference in the NMs and the sensing unit used. So that the simultaneous use of AuNPs and specific antibiotic aptamer for electrode modificatioin led to a 1.25 × 10 6 times lower LOD.

Yao et al. [64] suggested an innovative aptasensor for specific recognition and determination of KNM. In this work, the synthesized amine-functionalized MOF NC (UiO-66-NH2) was employed for the embellishment of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) corrected with MWCNT, rGO, and covalent organic framework (COF). COF was prepared using melamine-cyanutic acidmonomers complex (known as MCA) via polycondensation procedure. The developed nanohybrid had numerous amino groups and active sites on its surface that attributed to the presence of UiO-66-NH2/MCA. The high specific surface area and outstanding electrical conductivity were also given by MWCNT@rGO, which had a core-shell hetero-structure. Therefore, due to the ideal electrochemical activity and functional chemical groups-rich structure of nanohybrid, the complementary DNA (cDNA) strands terminated with the amino group, can simply anchor over the modified-GCE surface or penetrate within the porous network of UiO-66-NH2/MCA/MWCNT@rGO NC. Thereby, the suggested aptasensor showed a broad LDR of 25 to 900 nM and a very low LOD = 13 nM. In one of the latest studies, Geng et al. [65] expanded a MIP-based electrochemical sensor for CAP identifying in milk samples. In this project, applying an electro-polymerization method, the MIP was introduced on GCE modified with chitosan/MWCNTs for giving particular recognition sites for sensing CAP. Here, the authors used CAP as a template molecule and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as a functional monomer to synthesize the MIP material. During the electro-polymerization process, o-PD was deposited onto the electrode surface via electrochemical reactions, while CAP molecules were entrapped within the o-PD matrix through weak intermolecular forces (i.e., hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions). Once the CAP molecules were removed, the remaining cavity provided information about the original CAP shape and size, allowing for specific CAP detection. MWCNTs can also provide some advantages like high surface area and ideal electronic conductivity; hence, it was employed with the purpose of improving the electrochemical performance by minimizing the assay time, expanding LDR, and enhancing sensitivity. Meanwhile, it had a low LOD (3.3 × 10−2 ng/mL), and broad LDR (00.1–1000 ng/mL).

Screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) has commonly been employed in electrochemical biosensors due to its high performance, simple fabrication, cost-effectiveness, and miniaturization. In this respect, Hu et al. [66] proposed an electrochemical aptasensing transducer platform using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) method for the identifying of ciprofloxacin (CPF) based on a SPCE modified with CNTs-V2O5-chitosan NCs. Here, SPCE was used as a basal electrode for coating the CNT-V2O5-chitosan NC and immobilization of the single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) probe. The inclusion of V2O5 NPs to the CNT improved the conductive and catalytic attributes of SPCE. Undoubtedly, the sensitivity/selectivity of aptasensor can be improved by combining the merits of the biocompatibility of V2O5, portability of SPCE, the efficient electrical conductivity of CNTs, and proper film-forming ability of chitosan. The immobilization of anti-CIP aptamer onto the electrode surface led to significant decline in current response becsuse of aptamer complex structure. As such, the CIP detection was attended by a minor reduce in the current response, clearly showing successfully modification of the electrode surface. Under optimal experimental parameters, the designed biosensor demonstrated a LOD = 0.5 ng/mL over a LDR from 0.5 to 8.0 ng/mL.

In another report, KNM and streptomycin (STP) were determined by a dual-target electrochemical aptasensor synthesized based on the modification of SPCE with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (as signal tracers), specific target aptamer, and carbon-based NCs [67]. The NC was prepared using Graphitized MWCNT and CNFs-AuNPs (MWCNTGr/CNFs-AuNPs) that act as signal amplification. Indeed, it can improve the particular surface area of the modified electrode to immobilize a more amount of ANBs aptamer and enhancing the electrochemical response. When KAN and STP are present, they facilitate an interaction between the antibiotic and aptamer, leading to the disruption of double-strand DNA and subsequent changes in the concentration of Ca2+ and Pb2+ ions, which correspond to increased peak current values. Due to the excellent charge transfer properties of CNFs-AuNPs and MWCNTGr, the charge transfer rate of the sensor was significantly enhanced. The aptasensor provided a low LOD = 74.50 and 36.45 pM for KNM and STP, respectively, and showed ideal selectivity for both ANBs.

In one of the latest studies by Hu et al. [68], an electrochemical platform for sensing PNC-G, oxacillin, AMX, and AMP was developed using an GCE modified with AuNPs/Fe3O4-MWCNTs based on the screening and identification of DNA aptamers for PNCs. Here, the broad-spectrum aptamers were screened by graphene oxide-SELEX and were successfully obtained after 13 rounds of screening with high affinity and specificity. In the designed aptasensor, the aptamer folded to form a 3D spatial structure on the electrode surface to secure them tightly to the electrode surface and provide selective capturing and sensing sites for the PNCs and, hindering electron transfer and leading to celoined DPV response signals. Due to the excellent conductivity of MWCNTs/Fe3O4, the current peak was significantly enhanced after adding it to the electrode surface. Sulfhydryl modified aptamer was immobilized on the electrode surface by gold sulfur bonds, preventing electron transfer due to the electrostatic repulsion between aptamers, which led to reduce in current signal. When PNCs are present, the aptamer combined with the analyte to create a complex, blocking electron transfer, resulting in further reduction of current peak. As the AuNPs/Fe3O4-MWCNTs provide excellent electrochemical signal amplification strategy and amplifing subtle signal changes, the designed sensor achieved a lower LOD (0.667 nM) and a wider inear range (2–10,000 nM).

The presence of weak irreversible redox peaks on bare elecrode surface indicated a weak electron transfer ability and lower accumulation of target ANBs due to low surface properties. While, decoration of working electrode with CNTs materials lead to higher sensitivity of the redox peaks due to their excellent electro transfer peroperties. Further, a decrease in over-potential can also be noticed with CNTs due to their catalytic ability. In MWCNT-based electrochemical platform, functionalization of MWCNTs with nitric and sulfuric acid can increase their surface functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl and carboxyl groups), leading to more surface area and hydrophobic tubes via opening some of the end caps [57,69]. As such, MWCNTs can display potent adsorption effecticveness toward target ANBs owing to cation exchange between the functional group of ANBs and the H+ on the MWCNTs surface. Further, the interaction between a π-donor compound i.e., the hydroxyl group of CNTs and a π-acceptor compound in ANB structure can indeed enhance the adsorption of target ANBs onto MWCNTs surface. But, the potent adsorption layer could potentially diminish electrical conductivity. Thus, the addition of a complementary NMs (like metal and metal oxide NPs) to lower resistance would be necessary for the MWCNTs [61,69].

Graphene nanostructures (GNs) were obtained by the Geim and Novoselov for the first time in 2010 [70] . G is a single, flat layer of 2D one-atom-thick sheet with a trigonal lattice structure of carbon units that have a sp2 hybridization. In contrast to CNTs, the layer dimensions are not predetermined; however, they are frequently >500 nm. The carbon‑carbon connection longitude is also determined at 0.142 nm. GNs as one of the new emerging CNMs have gained scientists' attention due to their diverse unprecedented characteristics [71,72]. In this section, the current applications of GNs-based sensors for the determination of ANBs in milk and dairy products are presented.

In one study [73], an electrochemical sensor based on MIP was designed by chitosan-G NCs along with dual functional Au@Fe3O4 NCs (AuNPs and Fe3O4 magnetic NPs) for sensing KNM in spiked milk. KNM aptamers acted as the bind unit to KNM species and β-cyclodextrin-ferrocene, which served as a signal unit for KNM quantification and can be used for further modification of Au@Fe3O4. When the aptasensor was immersed in anti-KNM aptamer solution, the tracing tag captured on the surface by the KAN due to the high affinity of the aptamer for KNM. Owing to the bound ferrocene molecules in the sulfhydryl modified β-cyclodextrin cavities on the tracking label, the aptasensor indicated a current peak, and its intensity was correlated with the number of the KNM molecules rebound on the MIP. Reportedly, determination of KNM was proportionate to the released ferrocene. The sensor disclosed supreme sensitivity toward KNM with a LOD =1.87 nM (LDR: 10–500 nmol/L, and recovery rate: 101.5%) after 45 min of incubation time.

Jafari et al. [51] developed a cloxacillin (CLX) templated MIP-based voltammetric sensor using methacrylic acid as a practical monomer. MIP for CLX was synthesized by polymerizing CLO, MAA, and ethylene glycol di-methylacrylate (EGDMA) as cross-linker, while non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was prepared without CLO following a similar procedure. The pH of the solution and contact time are of the main affecting factors on the adsorption and detection process. MAA has generallya pKa around 4.65. In acidic conditions (>4.65), COOH groups become COOH2+ with positive charge. Due to the CLX's positive charge in acidic condition, this led to reducing CLO adsorption on MIP due to repulsive forces. However, in a neutral condition, COOH changes to COO−, promoting interactions between CLX and MIP, resulting in the most effective absorbtion in mildly acidic and neutral conditions. During adsorption, CLX molecules interacted with specific sites on the MIP surface over time but prolonged contact post-equilibrium may lead to weakly bound molecules being replaced, resulting in no significant efficiency change. Further, by increasing the MIP content in the solution, the CLX's adsorption efficiency steadily improved. The sensor was able to detect CLX with a LDR from 110 to 750 nM and LOD = 36 nM. This could be due to the electrode modification using GO and AuNPs, which increase surface area and enhance electron transfer along with the catalytic effect of AuNPs. Moreover, pre-concentration of CLX with MIP before quantification using the sensor could contribute.

In a study by Yu et al. [74], an electrochemical platform for sensing KNM and STP was developed using an SPCE modified with rGO and AuNPs on four channels. The KNM and STP aptamers were created by using two channels as working channels, and the remaining ones operated as calibration channels. The proposed potentiometric aptasensor exhibited LOD = 5.24 and 9.66 pM for KNM and STP, respectively, and was successfully applied to simultaneous determination of KNM and STP in milk samples. The obtained findings confirmed that the electrode surface indicated significantly more smoothness and uniformity after the rGO modification. Moreover, the membrane modification with rGO can improve electron transfer to the SPCE channel surface because of its great electronic conductivity and high surface area.

Sulfamethazine was detected by a novel electrochemical sensor produced based on the modification of GCE with GO embellished with Cu/Ag core-shell NPs (GO-Cu/Ag@GCE) [75]. The satisfactory electrochemical ability of GO-Cu/Ag@GCE platform was a synergistic effect of high electrical conductivity of Cu/Ag core-shell and excellent electron transportation of GO nanosheets. In optimized conditions, this sensor showed a concentration range between 10 and 1000 mM with a LOD = 0.46 mM. For the fixing of ANB-peculiar aptamers which increase the sensitivity of the biosensor, the mixing of two NMs, rGO and AuNP, offers better electrochemical activity, greater surface area, more electrical conductivity, and higher self-assembly sites. Mohammad-Razdari et al. [76] proposed an innovative apta-platform using the EIS technique for detecting TTC by pencil graphite electrode (PGE) corrected with rGO/AuNPs NC. Herein, the rGO/AuNPs NC was specifically developed to impart great electrical conductivity, high surface area, high electrochemical efficiency, and powerful bio-affinity toward PNC-G purposed aptamers to receive a very low LOD = 3 × 10−3 fM. As it self-assembled with AuNPs, the produced thiolated aptamer efficiently adsorbed on the electrode surface. Thereby, the proposed aptasensor provided ultrasensitive assay along with excellent selectiveness over similar structure ANBs such as STP, sulfadiazine, and PNC-G.

Similarly, an impedimetric aptasensor was developed based on PGE correction with AuNPs/rGO NC for ultrasensitive determination of PNC-G in different milk samples. The electron transfer rate was enhanced by immobilizing AuNPs/rGO on the PGE surface, leading to a notable increase in peak current for the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- compared to untreated PGE. Due to the electrostatic repulsion between negative charge of electrode and the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-, thiolated aptamer self-assembly on AuNPs/rGO@PGE surface led to reduced peak current for both aptamer and PNC-G compared to bare PGE. EIS was employed for PNC detection through measuring the charge transfer resistance (Rct). After modification of PGE with AuNPs/rGO, Rct value declined due to improved conductivity and electron transfer. Aptamer binding led to a negatively charged layer. However, after PEN incubation, Rct surged possibly due to PNC-G binding at aptamer/AuNPs/rGO@PGE sites. Accordingly, a brilliant PNC-G detectability was obtained by this aptasensor which was found to offer a wide LDR from 1.0 fM to 10 μM and a low LOD = 0.8 fM with proper selectivity toward analogous ANBs (STP, TTC, and sulfadiazine) [77].

An easy and sensitive definition procedure for KNM detection in fresh milk samples was proposed based on the modification of GCE with GO decorated with diblock DNA with 15-mer of poly-cytosine (poly-C) [78]. MWCNTs with a diameter of 20–40 nm were also immobilized on the GCE surface as an electrochemical signal amplifier. The diblock DNA was then deposited on GO/MWCNTs@GCE nanocomplex according to the potent physical adsorption between the poly-C and GO. The other element for quickly binding of target analyte, was KNM-specific recognition aptamer. This aptamer can specifically bind to the target and change the electrochemical signal. Consequently, the developed cyclic voltammetry (CV) transduction method exhibited proper analytical features, including a wide concentration range (0.05 pM to 100 nM), very low LOD (0.0476 pM), and excellent selectivity toward several interfering ANBs (AMX, CAP, STP, and OTC).

CAP was determined by an electrochemical sensor developed via a SPCE labeled with Sr-ZnO@rGO NC [79]. These NCs were fabricated based on the nano-sphere strontium (Sr) doped ZnO over rGO by the hydrothermal method. The NC created by this method has special qualities such as spherical shape onto rGO layers as well as large surface area for performance improvement. Sr-ZnO provided also improved sensing properties including good electrocatalytic activity, high electron transfer, and large specific surface area toward target ANBs. The Rct value was significantly lower for Sr-ZnO@rGO/SPCE (150.1 Ω) than for bare SPCE (1296.2 Ω), showing enhanced conductivity. This was due to the unique properties of Sr-ZnO@rGO, such as active functional groups and high surface area, resulting in high capacitance and low resistance. Further, bare SPCE had a current response at 60.67 μA, while Sr-ZnO@rGO/SPCE was 121.6 μA, showing improved redox peak response due to the well-coordinated arrangements of rGO NC with Sr-ZnO. Here, CAP was detected over the synthesized electrode (Sr-ZnO@rGO/SPCE) with a LOD = 0.131 μM and a LDR of 0.190 to 410.84 μM and 653.06 to 2847.14 μM. Moreover, the sensor demonstrated good sensitivity (1.0554 μA.μM−1.cm−2), satisfactory recoveries (97–99.8%), and excellent selectivity toward other interferences.

In a recent study, Kumar al. [80] fabricated a novel electrochemical sensor based on strontium titanate (SrTiO3)/GO nanocomposite for the detection of CAP in milk and honey samples. Here, SrTiO3 NPs were prepared via hydrothermal method and then attached to GO (SrTiO3@GO) through ultrasonication technique. The bare GCE indicated a minimal reduction peak, characterized by a peak current and a peak potential of −65.21μA and − 0.12 V, respectively, showing poor catalytic activity. The modification of SrTiO3@GO onto GCE led to a restrained improvement of the peak current (peak current; −105.53μA and peak potential; − 0.10 V) because of the excellent conductivity of NPs and their large surface area, which improved the interaction of target antibiotic on the GCE surface. The GCE coated with SrTiO3@GO nanocomposite also exhibited a notable reduction in Rct, measuring only 31.23 Ω. This decrease was linked to improved interactions with the electrolyte, stemming from higher porosity, better conductivity, and superior electron transfer efficiency and peak currents. Finally, the sensor was able to detect CAP with a LDR from 10 to 550 μM, LOD of 6.08 μM and a high sensitivity of 2.771 μA.μM−1.cm−2. A detailed description of the application of G-based sensor in ANB detection has been listed according to the detection technique, assay time, linear range and LOD in Table 2.

The adsorption or detection of antibiotics on graphene has shown impressive results due to the oxidization present within the graphene structure, resulting in the manifestation of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups. The adsorption process for removing or sensing antibiotics by graphene is multifaceted, relating not only to the surface characteristics of the graphene but also to the adsorption kinetics and isotherms, environmental conditions, dosage of adsorbent, and adsorption mechanisms [81]. To comprehend the sensing kinetics of antibiotics on graphene, one must first assess the dispersive state of the graphene NMs. GO with good dispersibility can adsorb antibiotics quickly, while graphene and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) with poor dispersion require more time to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Finally, the selection of graphene materials with good dispersing properties is crucial to achieving good electrochemical performance as soon as possible.

Significant efforts have been made to produce innovative types of CNMs because of their biocompatibility and effective adsorption capabilities [61]. In one report, Wong et al. [82] produced a sensitive electrochemical instrument for detecting AMX in milk based on the carbon black (CB) and cadmium telluride QDs. These QDs were included in a poly polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) film to correct the electrode (increase surface area) and improve the sensitivity of the sensor. Square-wave voltammetry (SWV) was applied to perform the electrochemical analysis of ANBs. A well-distributed range of QD sizes from 2 to 6 nm, along with the both π–π stacking interactions and hydrogen bond formation, which are present between hydrophilic -SO3 H group of PSS and hydroxyl or carboxyl groups of QD, leads to the formation of highly attractive sensing platform to bind AMX to surfcae of modified electrode via hydroxyl or amine group of AMX. In optimized situations, the sensor presented a low LOD = 0.05 nM, with a LDR from 0.90 to 25 μM. Therefore, this proposed sensor can be considered as a simple measurement device in pharmaceutical applications, clinical analysis, and food quality examination. In a research by Li et al. [83], a simple apta-platform for ultrasensitive recognition of KNM and STP was developed using CNFs and mesoporous carbon-AuNPs (CNFs@MC-AuNPs). For portability and on-site analyte detection, the prepared NC was conducted on a SPCE. Due to the rod-like structure of MC-AuNPs and CNFs, this sensor exhibited several unique properties, e.g. strong electrochemical conductivity and an ultra-large surface area. Therefore, the presence of ANBs in the milk samples was effectively detected by the CNFs@MC-AuNPs modified electrode and demonstrated a low LOD = 87.3 pM and 45.0 pM for KNM and STP, respectively.

Since the past decade, CB has attracted considerable interest in biosensor applications because of its cost-effectiveness, ease of deposition, large surface area, and high biocompatibility [84]. In this respect, Kulikovaet al. [84] fabricated a novel impedimetric aptasensor based on CB/chitosan NCs containing thiacalix[4]arene decorated with oligolactide fragments for the detection of KNM in milk and yogurt. The CB/chitosan/thiacalix[4]arene NCs were used as support to immobilize the aptamers on modified electrode using carbodiimide binding. The deposition of CB on the GCE surface led to enhance the effective surface area because of the inclusion of these conductive material in the upper layer. Exposure to KNM solution maintained peak potentials whereas enhancing currents, likely due to aptamer folding with KNM and shielding of negative charge of the phosphate residues in aptamer structure, reducing electrostatic repulsion for the redox indicator. Further, the detectable concentration of KNM reducced tenfold with the use of electrostatic assembly in the presence of a cationic polyelectrolyte. Accordingly, the presence of KNM significantly decreased the electron transfer resistance of the internal electrode interface with the low LOD = 0.3 nM and LDR from 0.7 and 50 nM. As a result, this proposed aptasensor was used for successfully KNM detection in real food, with recovery rates between 95 and 115%.

Metal-oxide NPs (MoNPs) have great potential in development and/or improvement of sensing technologies in order to meet the requirement for more precise and extremely sensitive bio-molecular diagnostics. MoNPs have attracted unprecedented attention for fabrication of different nano-devices, since they are efficient substrates for the immobilization of proteins, antigens, antibodies, and enzymes [85–87]. The versatility of MoNPs lies in their performance to create strong bonds with the working electrode via various methods like physical adsorption, electrodeposition, chemical covalent bonding, or even electopolymerization. MoNPs play an important role in electroanalytical uses through improvement of the conductive sensing interface, leveraging the catalytic attribures of NPs to enlarge the sensory range, and seting up electrical contacts between redox centers in sensig units and transducer surface, thereby enabling analytes comminucation between biomolecular components and sensing devices [88,89] . More so, in the presence of MoNPs, the rapid electron mobility between the transducer and target analytes is facilitated owing to their unique nanosized structure and composition, effectively rendering them as “electronic wires” and “electrocatalysts,” accelerating signal transmission and amplifying sensitivity. As such, by strategically incorporation of functionalized componenents into MoNPs warrants effective detection of target molecules such as DNA hybridization and protein binding events like antigen-antibody complex formation, ensuring accurate and reliable sensing outcomes [90,91]. Oxides of different metal elements such as iron, titanium, zinc, tungsten, etc., are such MoNPs being substantially investigated for increasing LODs of biosensors [24,87]. The recent application of various MoNPs-based sensors for the detection of ANBr in dairy products are summarized below.

Li et al. [92] applied metallic NCs based on Pt NPs, Sn NPs, and TiO2 nano-rods (Pt/SnNPs@TiO2) and exonuclease assisted target recycling to develop an electrochemical aptasensor for detecting STP. Accurate EIs studies showed a low LOD = 0.02 nM with a wide LDR of 0.05–1500 nM toward STP. The researchers reported that the DNA with a complementary sequence to the STP aptamer was immobilized effectively over a GCE modified with Pt/SnNPs@TiO2, and a target aptamer that hybridized with cDNA was then added. Reportedly, oxygen reduction current reduced by 51% due to hindrance from biomolecules. Introduction of STP and exonuclease resulted in cleavage of anti STP-aptamer complex and cDNA from GCE, enabling Pt/SnNPs surface for oxygen reduction. Exonuclease released STP for re-complexing with aptamers on GCE surface, leading to 85% cyclic amplification of oxygen reduction current linked to STP. Aghajari and Azadbakht [93] designed an anti-STP aptamer for effective sensing of STP in commercial milk samples with a LDR from 0.1 to 1500 nM and LOD = 18 pM. In this study, the authors modified GCE with chitosan-CNT decorated with palladium (Pd) NPs to make sure effective immobilization of anti-STP aptamer through π-π interaction. The electrodeposition of PdNPs at the surface of CNT/Chi@GCE resulted in the improvement of electron transportation ability between sensing platforms. The anodic current signal of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- significantly enhanced following the electrodeposition of PdNPs onto the CNT/Chi/GCE platform, confirming enhanced electron transfer between the sensing platform and the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- probe. In contrast, upon attaching anti-STP aptamer to the modified GCE, the peak current of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox couple remarkably declined, showing hindered diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- to the electrode surface. In accordance with results from the CV measurements, the Rct value for different GCE modification steps was recorded as follow; Chi@GCE > aptamer/PdNP/CNT/Chi@GCE > CNT/Chi@GCE > plain GCE > PdNPs/CNT/Chi@GCe, clearly confirming sucessfull fabrication of the aptasensor. The high selectivity and excellent affinity of aptamer/PdNPs/CNT/Chi@GCE platforms for STP detection rendered them ideal for a variety of applications in actual samples.

Manganese (III) oxide (Mn2O3) came across notable interest in electrocatalyst field own to their cost-effectiveness, excellent conductivity, and adjustable band gap. In this sense, a spongy and hierarchical Mn2O3 small nanostructures (Mn2O3@TNS) modified SPCE were applied for the determination of CAP in fresh milk samples. In this research, an easy sono-chemical method was used to synthesize Mn2O3TNS modified electrode via bath-type ultrasound washer [94]. Mn2O3 TNS corrected SPCE demonstrated the strongest prevalent answer to CAP when compared to plain SPCE. While the plain SPCE had higher semicircular and larger Rct value of 862.21 Ω, the Mn2O3 modified SPCE indicated the long linear portion at lower frequencies and the lowest Rct value of 269.39 Ω (frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz). This closely ascribred to the superior conductivity of nanosheets and better catalytic activity of the Mn2O3. In comparison, plain electrode displayd lower reduction peak current and higher potential. After modification with Mn2O3, the catalyst boosted active site availability, increasing reduction peak current and reducing overpotential to −0.64 V. Accordingly, the authors found that the sensor exhibited very low LOD = 4.26 nM and acceptable recovery rates (95.2–97.4%) for analysis of CAP in milk samples under optimized conditions. In another study, the interdigitated array microelectrodes (IDAMs) modified with antimony SnO2NPs-chitosan (SnO2NPs-Chi) film was designed and successfully applied for the electrochemical discovery of TTC in milk samples [95]. By electrostatic interaction of the non-covalent bond Au-NH2, the SnO2NPs can be decorated onto the microelectrode surface. The SnO2NPs incorporated in chitosan film was used to speed up the electron transfer, to effectively immobilize TTC aptamer onto the IDAM surface, and finally to improve the electrochemical performance. Herein, TTC was efficiently detected over the LDR of 10−10 to 10−3 g/mL and LOD = 3 ng/mL using EIS technique.

Reportedly, Yadav et al. [96] fabricated an electrochemical platform using cobalt oxide decorated rGO (Co3O4@rGO) for sensitive discovery of CAP in milk powder and packaged milk. A broad irreversible peak related to CAP reduction was recorded at bare GCE and GCE@Co3O4, with peak potentials at −0.75 V and − 0.73 V, respectively. In contrast, GCE@rGO and GC@Co3O4/rGO indicated a distinct irreversible reduction response at −0.56 and − 0.54 V, respectively, showing the reduction of CAP's nitro group to hydroxylamine via a four-electron process. This reduction occured at a more negative potential on these electrodes compared to others. Notably, CAP undergoes reduction at 210 mV more positive potential on the GCE@Co3O4/rGO than on the bare GCE. With increasing CAP concentration, a slight negative potential shift occurs possibly due to proton involvement in the rate-determining step, leading to pH changes near the electrode (decrease in H+ concentration), leading to the slight potential shift. The LOD and sensitivity of CAP detection were determined to be 0.55 μM and 1.32 μA.μM−1.cm−2, respectively. The wide LDR from 1 to 2000 M was also observed on the calibration curve for the detection of CAP using the CV method.

Due to the excellent catalytic activity, biocompatibility, specific surface property, and non-toxic nature, ZnO exhibited a promising potential for developing a variety of electrochemical sensors. In a study by Sebastian et al. [97], NCs of GO/3D flower-like ZnO (GO@ZnO MoNPs) were applied to synthesize an innovative electrochemical sensor for detecting CAP residue in the actual samples. Using a simple sonochemical procedure, the ZnO structure containing nano-sheets was combined with GO. Detailed CV studies indicated a high sensitivity of 7.27 μA.μM−1.cm−2, a low LOD = 0.01 μM, and a LDR from 0.2 to 7.2 μM toward CAP. The synergism of GO and hierarchical ZnO nano-sheets led to significantly higher electrocatalytic activity of NC-corrected electrode. Guan et al. [98] fabricated a flower-like ZnO/rGO/AuNPs-based aptasensor using the EIS technique for detecting TTC, doxycycline, and OTC in milk. The floral ZnO exhibited large specific surface area, excellent biocompatibility, and excellent electrochemical reactions. Also, rGO was used to improve the conductivity of ZnO. Next, the ZnO/rGO/AuNPs combination was employed to enhance the GCE's capacity for transporting electrons while providing an adequate number of active sites for aptamer capture. The proposed sensor exhibited low LODs of 0.33, 0.28 and 0.3 ng/mL toward TTC, doxycycline and OTC, respectively.

In a recent study [99], carbon paste electrode was modified with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and Mn3O4 nanostructures (g-C3N4/Mn3O4@CPE) for the detection of sulfathiazole (SMZ) and sulfapyridine (SPY) in real samples. g- C3N4 improved electrocatalytic activity by creating active functional sites via interaction with NPs through electron pairs within nitrogen vacancies, thereby modifing its surface. The redox activity of g- C3N4 was also heightened when combined with Mn3O4 NPs, leading to the trapping of electrons and prevention of recombination with the holes in g-C3N4. Indeed, the combined impact of Mn3O4 and g-C3N4/CPE enhances electron transfer and boosts sensor sensitivity. SMZ and SPY indicated oxidation peaks at 0.87V and 0.85V, respectively, without a reduction peak during the reverse scan could be due to the irreversible oxidation of the amine group in the sulfonamide ANBs. Further, the increase of anodic peak currents on the final modified CPE surface with the potential shift to less positive values clearly exhibited that the combination of g-C3N4 and Mn3O4 NPs facilitated the electron transfer kinetics by promoting the oxidation reaction of SMZ and SPY on g-C3N4/Mn3O4@CPE. Under the optimized experimental parameters, the sensor indicated broad LDR (SMZ; 0.75–100 μM and SPY; 1–100 μM), providing a low LOD = 0.11 and 0.23 nM for SMZ and SPY, respectively.

Due to their unique physicochemical properties, such as enormous surface area, simplicity, and distinctive spectral/optical properties, noble-metal NPs (NMNPs) have lately been employed for building bio-sensing platforms or improving bio-sensing methodologies [100,101]. Additionally, researchers have looked into Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR), as one of their distinguishing nano-scale characteristics, to create new NSs. They can range in size from 1 to 100 nm, have varied forms, and includes noble metals, iron, and heavy metals. NMNPs (particularly AuNPs and AgNPs) are among the most extensively studied NMNPs and have recently become a prerequisite for the development of common NSs [100,102]. This section will concentrate on the most current developments in NMs/NPs-based optical/electrochemical sensors used to identify ANBr in dairy products (Tables 2 and 3 ).

AuNPs are commonly employed in a variety of biosensors because of their improved conductivity, excellent absorptivity, large surface area and the ability to act as nanocarriers. Likewise, Song et al. [103] designed an electrochemical aptasensor for KNM sensing based on the assembly of cDNA strands of KNM aptamer and AuNPs. They employed the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) approach. Further, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was also employed as a biocatalyst for signal amplification. Accordingly, AuNPs demonstrated high biocompatibility, allowing for simple combinations with other biomolecules. Through Au-thiol interactions, the cDNA was hybridized with KNM aptamers immobilized on an Au electrode. Then, HRP catalyzed the reaction between hydroquinone and H2O2 in solution to produce a voltammetric signal. A wide LDR between 0.010 and 150 mg/L and a LOD =0.005 mg/L for KNM were obtained under optimal circumstances. When KNM was detected in milk samples in the presence of four analogous structures containing STP, TTC, OTC, and CAP, the proposed aptasensor showed good anti-interference capabilities.

Tian et al. [104] constructed a ratiometric sensing platform for identifying KNM in real milk samples for the purpose of verifying its efficacy in practical uses. The KNM-aptamer complementary and methylene blue labeled hairpin DNA were immobilized on the aptasensor together with streptavidin-functionalized CoFe2O4 nanozyme (as signal amplification materials). Vanadium disulfide (VS2)/AuNPs NC was used to support the electrode surface of this aptasensor, increasing its sensitivity, selectivity, and repeatability. Next, VS2/AuNPs@GCE was modified by linking sulfhydrylated MB-hDNA through the Au − S bond to create a double-strand structure with the anti-KNM aptamer via hybridization. In the presence of KNM, the aptamer binding led to a reduction in nanozyme accumulation and a rise in methylene blue response levels. The suggested sensor demonstrated a strong LDR from 1 pM to 1 μM and an extremely ultralow LOD =0.5 pM. The powerful electronic conductivity of VS2/AuNPs can be responsible for the great sensitivity.

Additionally, a novel apta-platform for sensitive and selective PNC sensing based on PNC aptamer (as a bio-recognition element) was developed [105]. Accordingly, in order to improve the electron transfer rate of AuNPs, they were electrodeposited onto an electrospun CNF mat for effective immobilization of PNC aptamer. More active sites for the immobilization of the aptamer can be provided by the placement of AuNPs (as a surface modification) onto the CNF electrode, leading to increased in the voltammetric response of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox and improved electrochemical performance. Further, a remarkable reduction was observed in the rate of charge transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- and the electrode surface after immobilizing PNC aptamer and target PNC onto AuNPs/electrospun CNF mat electrode, which closely related to barrier properties of PNC aptamer and PNC for the charge transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- and the modifed electrode. The developed aptasensor demonstrated a low LOD =0.6 ng/mL over a broad LDR of 1–400 ng/mL toward PNC in milk samples. Additionally, there was good consistency between the recoveries found in this study (98.2–102.5%) and those obtained using the HPLC technique (98.5–103.5%).

Zhang et al. [106] used a GCE improved with AuNPs that were electrochemically deposited on a PEI-functionalized Fe-based MOF (AuNPs/P-FeMOF@GCE) as a signal-amplification NC to construct a novel electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of tobramycin. As regard to the Zhang et al. (2021), two DNA probes were separately immobilized on the AuNPs/P-FeMOF@GCE sensing matrix as bio-recognition probes that were introduced according to the following; i) a signal producer probe; tagged with methylene blue at the 3′-end (MB-Probe); and ii) detection probes; short cDNA strands of both the aptamer and the MB-Probe label with a sulfhydryl group at the 5′-end (F-Probe), which engaged in competition with TOB for aptamer binding. As a result, the TOB-aptamer binding prevented F-Probe from becoming hybridized on the surface of GCE. Therefore, a sizable current response was generated by hybridizing with MB-Probe. Accordingly, the addition of AuNPs to the P-FeMOF sensing platform enhanced the sensitivity, conductivity, and stability of this straightforward competitive aptasensor. The high specific surface area and good electron conductivity of AuNPs/P-FeMOF NCs further demonstrate the special ability of FeMOF-based NS to enhance the recognition performance of the sensing method. With a LOD = 56 pM (LDR; 100 pM to 500 nM), the aptasensor was effectively used to identify tobramycin in milk samples [106]. Li et al. [107] studied the detection of pefloxacin (PEF) using an MIP-based electrochemical sensing platform. This system presented a proper LDR (0.005–10 μM), an adequate LOD (0.80 nM), and high sensitivity (3.199 μA μM − 1). Here, a stable sensing platform for PEF detection was created through electropolymerizing a poly(pyrrole) MIP film onto a AuNPs/black phosphorus nanocomposite (BPNS-AuNPs@MIP) with PEF present on a GCE. AuNPs/BPNS were formed via ultrasonically assisted spontaneous redox without any additional reducing agent. BPNS-AuNPs greatly improve the stability and electrochemical performance of BP while providing a larger surface area to host additional imprinted sites for sensitive/selective analyte binding. Rct value decreased from 216.0 to 20.06 Ω after attachment of AuNPs/BPNS to the GCE surface, showing that AuNPs/BPNS possess accelerate the electron transport and good electrical conductivity. However, electropolymerization of PPy-MIP films onto AuNPs/BPNS@GCE led to increase in Rct value to 1212 Ω. After creating cavities and removing the template, Rct decreased to 863.2 Ω. The empty imprinted sites enhanced the transport properties of redox species. In contrast, NIP/BPNS-AuNPs@GCE (1418 Ω) indicated higher Rct owing to the non-conductive nature of NIP, which hinders electron transfer. PEF oxidation peak at bare GCE (0.9064 μA) increased to 1.593 μA after constructing AuNPs/BPNS@GCE and further to 28.38 μA after deposition PPy-MIP-film PPy-MIP-film modified electrode, which this enhancement was ascribed to the selective PEF detection and capture abilities of the PPy-MIP film.

Magnetic NMs (MNMs) have gained enormous attention for sensing applications due to their inherent exclusive properties such as simple production, cost-effectiveness, tailored physicochemical attributes, controllable size, large surface area, different crystal structures, proper mass transference, and biocompatibility with diverse biomolecules. MNPs are independent NPs containing magnetic elements in a nano-metric size at the range of 1–100 nm. MNPs have superparamagnetic nature below 50 nm size, with the highest performance around 10–20 nm. It has been reported that only some materials can possess magnetic behavior [61,86,108]. Iron, nickel, and cobalt, as well as other elements coupled with them, are used as materials that can display considerably different magnetic properties. Additionally, some substances (e.g., aluminum oxide and AuNPs) without magnetic characteristics at their normal dimensions, can display magnetic properties at the nanoscale [24,109]. However, ferric and ferrous oxide in Fe3O4 and Fe2O3 are the most extensively employed MNPs due to their great biocompatibility [52].

In comparison to non-MNP-based sensors, MNP-based sensors demonstrate a great number of merits including less noise, lower LOD, more sensitivity, wider LDR, and swifter sensing time. Moreover, MNPs are easily and chemically modified and prompted the fabrication of innovative multi-functional NCs for different NSs [61,86]. A polymer-based magnetic sensor based on MIP and MNPs (i.e., Fe3O4) was assembled in one such report by Zeb et al. [135] to create an electrochemical sensor for the detection of TTC in raw milk samples. Based on acrylic acid, ethylene-glycol-dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and 2, 2 azobisisobutyronitrile (ABIN), the MIP was constructed. The reactivity and stability of MNPs were enhanced using surface coverage with tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and then modified with other compounds like methacryloxypropyl-trimethoxysilane (MPS). Subsequently, MIP was coupled to the modified MPNs (Fe3O4/SiO2–C@C) and performed by polymerization and optimization amounts of TTC (as analyte), acrylic acid (template monomer), EGDMA (cross-linker), and ABIN (radical initiator). Finally, TTC detection was performed using MNP-MIP on the carbon paste electrode (Fig. 4 ). According to detailed SWV analysis, the results were; LOD = 1.5 × 10−7 M, the LDR of 5.0 × 10−7 to 4.0 × 10−5 M, and repeatability of 2.82%. The MNPs-MIP sensor also displayed good repeatability, high sensitivity, stability, and suitable selectivity (Table 2). These findings showed that, the structured biomimetic sensor is highly efficient for sensing TTC and can be successfully employed on commercial scales.

In a similar study, Song et al. [111] extended a simple and label-free electrochemical sensor to detect OTC that showed a LOD = 0.027 pg/mL with a concentration LDR of 0.005 to 1.0 ng/mL. Here, Fe3O4@mesoporous carbon NC was synthesized by calcining Fe (II)-based MOFs to construct a sensor. An effective immobilization skeleton was sustained to OTC aptamer strands in this strategy, which ultimately provided high selectivity and good sensitivity toward ANB through impedimetric studies. This was made possible by the high stability, chemical functionality, and potent bio-affinity of the Fe3O4@mesoporous carbon NC. In another recent study, Pakapongpan et al. [112] prepared a nano-hybrid using Fe3O4NPs embed N-doped G (Fe3O4@NG) and loaded it on MSPE for on-site detection of CAP and diethylstilbestrol. In order to minimize interferences, detection of two ANBs was performed using the reduction current response at Fe3O4@NG@MSP. NG offers excellent conductivite properies and fast electron transfer from target ANBs structures to the electrode, while Fe3O4@NG provided high active sites for both ANBs. The excellent electrocatalytic ability of the modified electrode is attributed to the large number of nitrogen groups and Fe3O4 on the surface of NG that can effectively interact with CAP and diethylstilbestrol. As such, this construct indicated a detection threshold of 10 and 6.5 nM for CAP and diethylstilbestrol, respectively. This outcome can be explained by the superior capacity of Fe3O4@NG and the effective electrocatalytic attributes of Fe3O4@NG on the target molecules. More so, the aromatic structure of these ANBs enables them to be adsorbed onto NG via π-π interactions, benefiting from strong electrostatic interactions or the creation of hydrogen bonds, amplifing the increased cathodic peak current.

The combination of MNPs with other NCs sometimes offers a synergistic effect due to their interactive spatial network and good charge transfer ability, which can clearly improve the electrochemical characteristics. In view of this, Guo et al. [113] suggested an innovative label-free aptasensor for PNC detection. It was performed using a modification of GCE with platinum and titanium nanoporous, G-Fe3O4, and MWCNTs-Fe3O4 (Gr-Fe3O4/MWCNTs-Fe3O4/PtTi) NCs. This nano-complex can provide excellent electrochemical signal amplification and an appropriate immobilization substrate. Based on the CV assay, both Gr-and MWCNTs-Fe3O4 MNPs could accelerate the electrical conductivity and particular surface region activity of the GCE. As a result, this aptasensor showed ideal sensitivity and selectivity toward PNC, so that the LDR was from 0.05 to 100 ng/mL with a LOD = 0.0253 ng/mL.

In a recet study, Serdar Sanli [114] extended a single-drop electrochemical immunosensor for onsite smartphone detection of that showed a LOD of 0.44 μM with a limited LDR of 0.5 and 100 μM. Here, sensing platform was based on anti-AMX antibodies conjugated amino-functionalized iron oxide magnetic NPs (MNP-TA) on SPE surface (Abd/MNP-TA@SPE). A 3D cell captured AMX molecules, interacted with antibody-coated MNP, and recognized AMX in raw milk with a smartphone-connected potentiostat. Peak heights rose post iron oxide MNPs and MNP-TA/AMX-Abd immobilization on the SPE because of the presence of positively charged amine groups. In contrast, peak height decreased following the interaction of Abd/MNP-TA@SPE and AMX, as electron transfer was hindered between AMX-Abd/MNP-TA@SPE and the studied solution. Further, decreased Rct values stated enhanced electron transfer owing to amine group interaction with Abd and redox probe. Conversely, increased Rct with AMX-Abd/MNP-TA@SPE indicated AMX coating hinders redox probe and electrode interaction, decreasing charge transfer at the SPE surface.

Anh et al. [115] fabricated a sensitive electrochemical sensor based on core/shell Ag@Fe3O4 NPs for identification of furazolidone (FZD). The electronic conductivity of the bare SPE and the Ag@Fe3O4/SPE was studied using the EIS measurement in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution. The clear drop in Rct value exhibited better redox species reduction at the electrode surface and interface, improving the electrochemical reduction efficiency at Ag@Fe3O4/SPE. In the absence of FZD, no redox peaks were detected. But, after FZD addition, a distinct reduction peak appeared in both bare SPE and Ag@Fe3O4/SPE. The irreversible reduction peaks likely stems from direct −NO2 group reduction to −NHOH group with a four-electron transfer, aligning with FZD reaction mechanism. This aligns with CV and EIS findings for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, showing that the modified electrode greatly enhanced electrochemical performance for FZD detection. Accordingly, Ag@Fe3O4/SPE exhibited a LDR from 15 to 100 μM, LOD = 0.24 μM, and good sensitivity (1.36 μA.μM−1.cm−2).

MOFs, first defined as a new fascinating type of porous and ordered crystalline coordination materials, have recently shown great potential in the development and applications of biosensors [116]. MOFs are precursor hybrid materials composed of metallic cores linked together by potent coordination bonds with organic bridging ligands, which finally lead to the organization of versatile extended networks. Given that, MOFs are composed of two fundamental inorganic/organic components; their architectures and physicochemical properties can be simply modified or adjusted by choosing the appropriate metal centers and organic ligands to provide the desirable benefits for the intended function [116,117]. Due to their inherent characteristics (wide surface area, high internal pore volumes, and highly tunable composition, structure and pore size (varying between angstroms and nanometers)), MOFs exhibit superior functional properties and have become one of the most attractive studied NMs in sensing fields [117,118]. In this part, the MOFs' ability to identify ANBr in milk samples are discussed below.

In a related study, Chen et al. [119] fabricated a new type of sensitive electrochemical bio-codes based on aptamer, Pb2+ and Cd2+, and nano-sized MOFs (NMOFs) for identification of KNM and CAP. In this work, Pb2+ and Cd2+ metal ions used as signal amplification to improve sensor selectivity toward target analytes. NMOFs were also served as substrate for loading enough amount of metal ions via the attachment of amino groups in pores, because they can offer an ultra-high specific surface area approximately to 1052 m2/g. Next, metal-NMOFs that contained the bio-codes specific to the tested ANBs were labeled using cDNA strands targeted aptamers toward those ANBs for obtaining processing signal tags. The aptamer functionalized magnetic beads were then joined with the labeled metal-NMOFs. Indeed, in the presence of magnetic beads, metal-NMOFs could be released quickly as a result of the conjugation of target analytes and sensor. This can surprisingly improve the assay process using the SWV technique. With low LOD = 0.16 and 0.19 pM, respectively, the developed aptasensors demonstrated great selectivity and sensitivity for the synchronous detection of KNM and CAP.

In subsequent study, Zhou et al. [120] developed a label-free aptasensor for OTC residues determination. This bio-platform was performed based on Ce-based MOF and porous COF NMs (namely Ce-MOF@COF nanohybrid) that were produced through the polycondensation of different amounts of melamine and cyanotic acidmonomers layers (Fig. 5A). These nano-hybrids exhibited a great number of merits, including potent hydrogen-bonding, electrostatic, and π-π interactions between aptamer strands and the porous Ce-MOF@COF frameworks. This can strengthen the stability of aptamer as well as OTC adsorption. The LOD and linear electrochemical response of this aptasensor was 0.000036 nM and 0.3–1.0 nM, respectively.

In one study, ultra-highly sensitive recognition of STP was obtained based on MOF-based bio-codes coupling with enzyme-assisted goal recovery for further signal enhancement [121]. In order to synthesize sensing platforms, SPCE surface was simply rectified with a mixed monolayer of 6-mercaptoethanol (MCH), complementary DNA (cDNA), aptamer duplexes (dsDNA) with -thiol terminus, and AuNPs (MCH/cDNA/dsDNA/AuNPs/SPCE) (Fig. 5B). Highly effective elimination of the aptamers from dsDNA with the help of the Exo I enzyme was done in the presence of the target STP. The electroactive {Ru(NH3)6}3+ adsorption (as a signal indicator) on SPCE surface was then created using backfilled MOF-based bio-bar codes. Indeed, the idea of using MOF as a nanocarrier for the immobilization of AuNPs was inspired by this procedure. This means, more DNA strands may be immobilized onto each Au/MOF NC and increasing the {Ru(NH3)6}3+ adsorption capacity. This aptasensor showed a low LOD = 2.6 pg/mL toward STP with a wide concentration LDR of 0.005 to 150 ng/mL.

In another study by Niu et al. [122], an electrochemical sensing platform for sensing FZD and CAP was developed using a GCE modified with Co/Ni-MOF derived hollow NiCo2O4@C composite. Reportedly, several unique attributes of NiCo2O4@C like hollow and mesoporous structure, proper carbonaceous frameworks, and sufficient oxygen defects led to providing a proper electrocatalytic performance for simultaneous detection of FZD and CAP (Fig. 5C). The proposed sensor indicated broad LDR (FZD; 0.5–240 μM and CAP; 0.5–320 μM), providing a low LOD = 8.47 and 35 nM for FZD and CAP, respectively. In another similar investigation by Peng et al. [123], Ti3C2 and MOFs modified Au electrode (Ti3C2/MOFs@GE) was constructed and used to detect STP for the first time. Herein, the Ti3C2/MOFs nanocomposite was prepared via an impregnation approach and the morphology and structure analysis confirmed that the mesoporous structure provides a great opportunity to capture and detection of STP in real milk samples. Under optimal experimental parameters, the proposed platform demonstrated a very low LOD =0.0033 nM over a LDR from 0.01 to 200 nM with satisfactory recoveries (94.0–105.0%), and excellent selectivity toward other interferences.

In one of the recent works, an innovative electrochemical aptasensor was offered by Hui et al. [124] for detecting STP in raw milk samples using various techniques. This work was performed based on the modification of the GCE surface using AuNPs/poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride)-modified N-doped CNTs and the MIL-101(Cr) functionalized with PEI (PEI-MIL-101(Cr)) using hydrothermal and ultrasonication methods (AuNPs/PDDA/N-CNTs/PEI–MIL-101(Cr)@GCE) (Fig. 5D). This platform provided a proper surface for the efficient immobilization of anti-STP aptamer via AuS bonds and π–π stacking interactions and subsequently current signal amplification. Accordingly, the authors found that the aptasensor exhibited low LOD = 2.31 nM and a broad LDR (0.01–250 nM) for rapid and sensitive analysis of STP in actual samples under the optimal experimental conditions.

MOFs have various different properties; they (i) exclude channels of certain sizes, or (ii) indicate host-guest interactions within their cavities, (iii) coordinate or form hydrogen bonds with target analytes, (iv) respond specifically to certain signals, and also (v) exhibit chirality due to their unique frameworks.
