Most important factors that supports the creation of MOF-baed electrochemical sensors is the strong interactions between MOFs functional groups and the target ANBs through electrostatic forces, stacking, or hydrogen bonding, reulting in accumulation of the target biomolecules. However, MOFs often have poor electrical conductivity due to their high proportion of organic ligands, which lead to a lower electrochemical detection performance. Therefore, due to the large surface area of MOFs materials, research studies have focused on various aspects to improve the electrical conductivity and strenghth the signals of MOF-based platform via combining them with other highly conductive NMs like metal or metal oxides NPs or carbon materials.

Dendritic macromolecules (dendrimers), also referred to a unique soft nano-containers, have recently become an appropriate prerequisite to the efficient encapsulation of supramolecular NPs for the facile construction of various nano-devices. They are high-branching and low-polydisperse macromolecules having diverse 3D or globular structures. Some recent reports confirm that dendrimers can be successfully used for the modification of electrode surfaces due to the increasing number of hydrophobic or electrostatic interactions. In this regard, they can improve the sensitivity, selectivity, and performance of specific electrochemical responses. Additionally, they have been proven as ideal substrate materials owing to their numerous active sites [125–127]. Poly(amidoamine) (represented as PAMAM) is the most often investigated dendrimer for the detection of ANBr due to its compact molecular structure and great number of functional surface groups (Fig. 6 ). However, the application of these apprised NMs in the electrochemical sensing platform has received little attention, possibly due to their non-conductive features. Therefore, they can be commonly combined with various types of NMs such as Ag/Au NPs, CNMs, MNMs, etc. for improving their electrocatalytic activity, electron transfer ability, surface properties, and electrochemical performance [126,127]. The following section summarizes current advances in dendrimers for the electrochemical identification of ANBr in milk samples.

Mahmoudpour et al. [126] offered a novel electrochemical aptasensor for detecting CPF in raw milk samples using the CV method. It was performed based on the assembly of 3D AuNPs-PAMAM/rGO on the GCE surface. In the first step, rGO was loaded on the electrode surface as a substrate that provides a lot of active functional groups. As such, prepared 3D AuNPs-PAMAM NC were covalently electrodeposited onto the rGO-modified electrode surface. The coupling of PAMAM with rGO and AuNPs exhibited some combination advantages like effective surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, enough active sites, and improved surface functionality of GCE for efficient affinity to CPF residue. As a result, the developed apta-platform showed ideal analytical characteristics, namely a limit of quantification (LOQ) = 1 nM over a concentration range of 1 mM−1 nM. Similarly, Liu et al. [128] suggested a PAMAM@Au-based electrochemical immune-sensor for efficient recognition and quantification of NFX. Herein, the PAMAM@Au NCs could enhance the electron transfer ability as well as the effective immobilization of antibodies on the GCE surface. In the competitive immune-sensor assay, the reaction of the substrate hydroquinone and H2O2 was catalyzed by the introduction of the HRP-tagged antigen (served as signal labels). Meanwhile, this sensing platform indicated satisfactory recoveries (91.6–106.1%), very low LOD (0.3837 μg/L), wide linearity (1 μg/L to 10 mg/L), and acceptable specificity toward different interferences (enoxacin, fleroxacin, sparfloxacin (SPX), and mariposide).

In another work, florfenicol was determined with the aid of a brand-new imprinted electrochemical sensor based on the GCE decorated by dendritic PtPd bimetallic NPs (poly-5-IAA) [129]. Poly-5-IAA were deposited onto poriferous N-doped G (N-P-r-GO)-modified GCE to create a porous and 3D nanostructure. Then, the 3D MIP, as a promising recognition receptor, was immobilized on Pt-PdNPs/N-rGO@GCE employing the polymer to elute the template molecule (florfenicol) for improving the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. Subsequently, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- was also employed for analysis of the electrochemical impedance signal for recognizing target ANBs. Reportedly, the poly-5-IAA has different functionsl groups such as −COOH, -NH, and a benzene ring, which enables π-π interactions with the florfenicol molecules in the MIP film. More so, the florfenicol also have groups of −OH, -S=O, and -NH, which facilitate multiple hydrogen bondings with the poly-5-IAA, leading to a higher affinity for the target analyrs. This leads to improved sensitivity and selectivity compared to the NIP sensor. The electrochemical response for florfenicol was favorable with a LOD = 1.0 × 10−9 M and linear extent from 5.0 × 10−8 to 8.0 × 10−6 M.

These studies indicated that the dendritic nanostructures with unique 3D or globular morphologies, large specific surface area, and numerous active sites are credited as promising substrates on the surface of electrodes in NSs. Indeed, dendrimer-encapsulated metallic NMs (in particularel PtPd bimetallic NPs, rGO, or AuNPs) have drawn scientists' attention in using of them as the bioaffinity electrochemical nanoprobes because of the high density of active groups, the excellent structural homogeneity, biocompatibility and well conductivity. However, it should be noted that the application of dendrimers-based sensors has not been widely investigated so far. As a result, the data suggested that more comprehensive research in this area is required. Some of the most common dendrimer -based sensors assessed in the published works for the detection of ANBr are summarized in this section (Table 2).

Polymeric NMs-based sensors have improved as bio−/nano-sensors due to their flexible and viable options [130]. They could be made from polyphenazine/polytriphenylmethane polymers, CNMs, metal NMs, QDs, and MNMs [52,131]. NMs are often fabricated in the form of prototype and play a great function in compounds identification and microorganisms detection [132]. One of these NSs are sulfide NPs amperometric sensor, designed by Montero et al. [132], and used as estimation method to identify the residues of CAP in meat instead of traditional methods such as chromatographic, and immunological methods. The action mechanisms of sulfide NPs in the detection of ANBs is the absorption of these compounds by trapping and fixing them on the surface [133]. It is desirable to use NPs to make sensors with a larger absorption surface to create more sensitivity [134]. Chen et al. [134] reviewed various functionality of Au/AgNPs with polymers as sensors, which have different advantages. Progresses in polymer-based NSs on fluorescence resonance energy transfer are able for detecting low levels of ANBs [135]. Herein, other application of polymeric NMs-based sensors as detection tools of different analytes are discussed.

Recently, investigations have focused on developing bio−/nano-sensors using a combination of NMs and polymers to enhance sensing performance, often in terms of selectivity and sensitivity [136]. Mainly, they consist of conjugated conducting polymers which represent a different tactic and redox polymers. There have been reports in the field of NMs-based electrochemical bio−/nano-sensors that, electropolymerization create redox polymer [137]. Redox polymer/NMs NCs have characteristics including simple production, variable shape, high electroactive surface area, chemical consistency, and outstanding redox characteristics, making them suitable transducers for electrochemical sensors [138]. Regrettably, the weak chemical/physical consistency of the enzymes or antibodies confined their usage in surroundings with bases or acids, organic solvents, and high temperatures whereas due to their great electrical, chemical, and physical characteristics, different conducting polymers are of attentiveness for biosensor usages and researchers have taken advantage of these characteristics to improve and correct the efficiency of these biosensors by assessing the structure of the conducting polymer in nano-scale including 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D [139].

Conducting polymer NPs are recognized for their compact proportions, large surface-to-volume ratio, and increased sensitivity that are frequently used as sensing transducers [139]. Despite these qualities, the weak structural consistency prevent typical conducting polymer NPs from being widely used [139]. Among these, polypyrrole (PPy) as a conducting polymer possessing five-membered heterocyclic rings can be mentioned [140]. The electrical and optical characteristics of the sensing transducer are influenced by the polyconjugated chains of conducting polymers, which consist of changing single (σ) and double (π) connections [141]. The length of the conjugation, the presence of intra- and inter-chain interactions, and the degree of disturbance in the system are some of the factors that affect how conducting polymers behave physically [142]. Additionally, studies have demonstrated that adding whole or partial of conducting polymer nanostructures to sensor transducers enhances their ability to sense [143].

The ability to selectively defects adjust, electronic states, and the surface chemistry have been the driving force behind diverse methods for fabricating morphologically tunable conducting polymer NMs [144]. Moreover, assembling conducting polymer nanostructures into an ordered array is frequently necessary to render them functional. When new methods for joining and assembling nano-scale components are developed, novel NMs-based sensing devices demonstrate expanded capabilities [145]. There are many reported synthetic techniques, however, the template method is the most potent and effective one for creating conducting polymer NPs. In this method, inorganic or organic guest molecules are inserted into the host material's vacancies. These voids by acting as the “template,” determine the composition's size, shape, and orientation. The template method is typically divided into three categories: hard, soft, and template-free methods [146,147]. Due to the quick rate of electron transfer, redox polymers have seen an increase in use recently in biofuel cells, biosensors, and energy storage technologies. They facilitate the alteration of the electron state of their active site by serving as a redox mediator [148]. Redox polymer electrocatalytic capabilities are integrated with the electrical, mechanical, and catalytic properties of different NMs to create electrochemical bio−/nano-sensors that are more sensitive and selective [131]. Among redox polymers, polyphenazine/ polytriphenylmethane polymers, poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT), which is a polythiophene (PT) derivative, and the conductive states of polyaniline (PANI), have seen extensive use in electrochemical bio−/nano-sensors over the past years [149]. Some of the most common polymeric nanomaterils -based sensors assessed in the published works for the detection of ANBr are summarized in this section (Table 2).

In complex matrices, the concentrations of ANB are often very low, or the ANB structures may be changed. Thus, reliable, quick, and accurate approaches for recognizing ANBrs are needed. On the other hand, further development of low-cost, fast, portable and environmentally friendly technologies for in-situ measurements is also very important. Optical sensing systems can efficiently interact with different biorecognition components of analytes and then turn them into visual optical outputs [53]. Over the past decade, NM-based ANB recognition has been developed in combination with colorimetric systems, Raman scattering, fluorescence spectroscopy, and surface resonance. In this regard, the use of various NMs to create optical sensors could indicate remarkable potential for reaching a very high sensitivity and low LOD.

The sensitivity/specificity recognition of ANBr is an undeniable issue. Besides, it should be noted that the amount of the sample should be as low as possible to reduce analysis contamination. Therefore, it is ideal to create more desirable strategies for routine detection that are not only very sensitive but also easy to use, practical on large scales, and inexpensive to implement. In this regard, NM-based fluorometric assays are widely recognized for their great sensitivity (Table 3).

Among the broad family of NM-based optical sensing systems, CNMs are a popular group. Several studies have established ANBr analysis based on CNMs. Also, many QDs and their derivatives have been successfully used as fluorescence probes to recognize ANBrs. As a novel class of CNMs, GQDs have been recently introduced as a shining star in fluorescence sensing systems because of their numerous advantages like ease of synthesis, excellent fluorescence modifying ability, water solubility, nontoxicity, biocompatibility, and huge surface areas. Li et al. (2018) developed a GQDs@Eu3+ ratiometric system (by combination of GQDs and europium (Eu3+)) for sensitive determination of TTC with ratiometric luminescence change. The prepared system could quench fluorescence when TTC is applied. In this regard, TTC was added to the system, and as a result, both GQDs and Eu3+ luminescence was modified, and a ratiometric signal was acquired. Additionally, the carboxyl agent on the edge and surface of the GQDs significantly contributed to strengthening Eu3+ fluorescence. The impedimetric aptasensor indicated efficient determination of PNC-G with a great LDR from 1.0 fM to 10 μM and a low LOD = 0.8 fM.

Wang et al. [150] synthesized a new and biocompatible fluorescent biosensor for highly sensitive detection of OTC based on carbon QDs (CQDs), H2O2, and Fe3O4 MNPs (CQDs-H2O2-Fe3O4 NCs) as fluorescence reporters (Fig. 7A). Here, the CQDs fluorescence reduced in the existence of ANBs, H2O2 and Fe3O4. It was shown that the inner filter impact between CQDs and studied analytes, as well as the hydroxyl radical generated by the Fenton reaction and intercalation of Fe3O4 with analytes, are all responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Excessive amounts of H2O2 in the reaction solution trigger a redox process involving the conversion of Fe2+ ions on the surface of MNPs to Fe3+, resulting in the hydroxyl radical's production. These highly reactive species then quench the fluorescence of CDs, resulting in diminished signal intensity. The combination of UV–Vis absorption spectra for OTC and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra for CDs reveals that adding OTC reduces CD fluorescence without altering its emission wavelength, implying possible quenching mechanisms owing to inner filter effect (IFE). Accordingly, this can enhance the specificity and sensitivity of this optical biosensor. In this project, OTC was successfully determined over the LDR of 25 nM to 1.75 μM and LOD = 9.5 nM.

An innovative fluorometric sensor has been lately suggested for the detection of TTC in milk and milk powder samples by Wang et al. [151]. This fluorescence nano-probe was constructed based on the tungsten oxide QDs (WxOy QDs) through bottom-up ethanol-thermal strategy. Under 365 nm UV light, WxOy QDs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence. This emitted blue fluorescence could be clearly quenched in the presence of TTC, which was closely linked to the synergism of IFE, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The quenching of fluorescent probes toward WxOy QDs can be attributed to IFE toward TTC, which arises when the absorbance of the quencher overlaps with the excitation of the fluorophore. After adding TET, the fluorescence lifetime shortened from 5.68 to 4.95 ns, suggesting that FRET can also contribute to the quenching mechanism alongside IFE in the reaction system caused by the interaction of TTC with the WxOy QDs. Decrease in fluorescence emissions was attributed to random collisions and interactions between the fluorescence nanoprobe and the naphthacene group skeleton of TTC. More so, the electrode potential of TTC (−1.44 eV) lies between the valence and conduction bands of WxOy QDs, showing the presence of PET. The results indicated that PET (quenching efficiency; 43.26%) was the dominant quenching mechanism, followed by IFE and FRET. Due to the naphthacene group skeleton present in the TET molecule, FRET was also observed to be more pronounced compared to IFE. To sum up, the good linear relationship and low LOD were calculated as 0–50 μmol/L and 0.019 μmol/L, respectively.

Ahmed et al. [152] suggested a label-free fluorescence aptasensor based on GQDs/PdNPs for detection of TTC in real samples. In this study, the direct one-step fabrication of GQDs from graphite with l-ascorbic acid was done. A novel one-step process was also established to produce non-spherical Pd NPs aplying 3,3′,5,5′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (Fig. 7B). PdNPs acted as a fluorescent quencher of GQDs in the existence of TTC-targeted aptamers. With the addition of target TTC (specific interaction between aptamer and TTC), the fluorescence emission increases with the release of GQDs, which is related to the nonspecific and weak aptamer-GQDs interaction. The release of GQDs directly and the amount of TCs indirectly are related to the fluorescence emission. As such, the fluorescence emission measurement presented a low LOD = 45 ng/mL (LDR; 40–90 ng/mL).

For detecting KNM in actual milk samples, Wang et al. [153] lately created an inventive fluorescent aptasensor based on the DNA template/silver nano-clusters (DNA-AgNCs) and AuNPs. This particular DNA template was composed of aptamers, thymine linkers, and cytosine-rich fragments that can effectively emit fluorescence in the visible red wavelength region. Herein, electrical double layers were formed between DNA and AuNPs. The distance between DNA-AgNCs (energy donor) and AuNPs (energy acceptor) was shortened as a result of electrostatic interaction causing DNA binding with AuNPs. Controlling DNA template and AuNP particle size enables optimal overlap between DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs' UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra, enhancing detection capabilities. FRET occurs between DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs, potentially leading to quenched fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs. Adding KNM disrupted electrostatic interactions between DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs, which is likely due to the strong affinity between aptamer and KNM. As such, there was a decrease in the FRET between DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs, and the intensity of the DNA-AgNC fluorescence signal was also restored, allowing detection of KNM. Reportedly, the developed sensor displayed good selectivity and sensitivity with a very low LOD = 22.6 nM over a LDR of 100–1100 nM.

In general, MOFs have been introduced as appropriate fluorescent NMs that could be effectively quenched by target analytes. Furthermore, it has been reported that adding various functional groups, such as -NH2, to organic linkers can strengthen the tenability of MOFs and give a more active site as well as more potential for electron transportation toward target analytes [117,154]. In this respect, Li et al. [154] synthesized a fluorometric sensor for detecting TTC by MIL-type MOFs consisting of Al3+ prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal polymerization method (Fig. 7E). This fluorometric system had abundant NH2 and COOH groups in its crystalline nano-plate structure. The incorporation of functional groups (i.e., NH2 and COOH) led to interaction with the CO/OH of target analytes to create a complex (via hydrogen bonding between NH2 and CO/OH). The authors reported that the specificity, sensitivity, and potential applications of MOFs for TTC analysis in real milk samples was obviously improved after incorporation of functionalized -NH2 groups. Therefore, this sensor had the properties of fast assay time (30 s), low LOD (26.16 nM), and acceptable recovery rates (between 85.2 and 112.1%), with 57–89% quenching efficiency of inner filter effect (IFE).

In another similar investigation, TTC residue was recognized using 3D terbium (Tb)-containing MOF [155]. In this study, the hydrothermal method was used to prepare sensing materials (Tb-MOF). The Tb-MOF was capable of emitting a potent green fluorescence with an extended fluorescence lifespan, which was sensitively and specifically quenched using TTC aqueous solution (Fig. 7C). Additionally, recyclable luminescence sensing during ANB recognition was also observed. The synergistic effect of IFE and PET from the ligand to the Tb3+ ion were the key inspiration factors for the luminescence quenching mechanism. Meanwhile, the results of TTC determination in raw and pasteurized milk samples indicated an extensive concentration LDR from 0 to 100 and 100–244 μmol/L, low LOD = 184 nmol/L, and satisfactory recovery between 93.5 and 104.2%. Khataeeet al. [156] developed a novel dual-emission fluorescence probe based on the simultaneous association of nano-clusters of gold and copper (AuCNs-CuNCs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) for ratio-metric visual TTC detection. Under a wavelength of 400 nm, the provided NC (AuNCs-CuNCs@ZIF-8) indicated dual emissions centered at 520 and 630 nm, which were created by AuNCs and CuNCs, respectively (Fig. 7D). Accordingly, TTC substantially reduced the red emission severity of the CuNCs at 615 nm, whereas the green emission of AuNCs at 520 nm remained almost steady. This sensing probe displayed a satisfactory response speed (5 min), wide LDR (20 to 650 nM), and LOD (4.8 nM) for TTC quantification in milk.

The same group later designed a dual-emission fluorescence probe using ZIF-8 decorated with green emitting carbon dots (gCDs) and AuNCs for the ratio-metric recognition of cephalexin (CEP) residues in milk samples [157]. This sensor displayed two emission peaks at 520 and 630 nm under stimulation at 400 nm. The authors approved that CEP selectively quenches the fluorescence of AuNCs (630 nm), but the fluorescence of gCDs (520 nm) nearly stays unchanged. The primary cause of the fluorescence quenching was the formation of CEP and BSA complexes on AuNCs surface. The fluorescence quenching of AuNCs in gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 was significantly higher than in single AuNCs when exposed to the same CEP content (63% vs. 94%). This enhanced sensitivity was ascribed to ZIF-8's high porosity and sorption ability, enabling analyte accumulation. Further, MOF rose AuNCs' fluorescence and improved sensor sensitivity. MOFs also offered selective sensing by restricting specific interfering CEP molecules with their small pores. The sensor presented excellent sensitivity toward CEP detection with a low LOD = 0.04 ng/mL due to the combination of the satisfactory substrate properties of ZIF-8 along with unique fluorescence features of gCDs and AuNCs.

In another report, Amiripour et al. [158] explored a fluorometric sensor for CAP recognition in real samples. In this study, nanostructured MIPs were merged into a luminescent zirconium MOF (MIP/Zr-LMOF). Herein, when a CAP template was present, the Zr-LMOF surface was altered using MIPs to create recognition binding sites for reacting with ANBr, which are then incorporated into the sensor. In this regard, CAP residue can be detected selectively and sensitively owing to the proliferous composition of Zr-LMOF, which has specialized binding locations for analyte identification. Stronger coordination of Zr nodes with the carbonyl group in CAP compared to 2-NH2-terephthalic acid (ATA) ligand weakened the ZrO node interaction with ATA, intensifying the vibration of the carbonyl group. This decreased the ligand–metal charge transfer effect upon ATA coordination with ZrO clusters, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. This optical sensor was capable of sensing CAP in the range of 0.16–161.56 μg/L (LOD = 0.013 μg/L) with high selectivity toward some analogous interferences such as TTC, thiamphenicol, ETC, gentamycin, enrofloxacin, NFX, florfenicol, and CPF. Some of the most common MOFs-based sensors assessed in the published works for the detection of ANBr are summarized in Table 3.

Colorimetric sensors for ANBr detection have been created because of their unique characteristics including easy experimental operation, quick and short response time, low cost, ideal accuracy, and portability. Colloidal and spherical NMNPs (e.g., AuNPs and AgNPs) are generally very understandable and acceptable for colorimetric sensor development. The color response of AuNPs or AgNPs solute relies on the aggregation and dispersion of NPs. Thus, NMNP-based colorimetric sensors frequently need biorecognizable elements or probes to promote NPs aggregation and dispersion.

To construct a novel bio-sensing assay for CAP, an AuNP nano-probe was assembled with hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme and cDNA strand [159]. This cDNA (i.e., oligonucleotide against CAP-aptamer) was sulfhydrylated and assembled to create the nano-probe. In other words, the aptamer and cDNA produced a hybrid without the addition of CAP and serving as a bio-sensing platform. A colorful material was constructed for the colorimetric signal transduction based on the catalytic reaction of the peroxidase-mimitating hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme. The addition of CAP, induced a reduction in color intensity due to the adhering of the target to the aptamer. Meanwhile, the red color of the substrate solution turned yellow owing to the catalytic activity. However, when the target was bound to the aptamer by the addition of CAP, the color intensity decreased. The aptamer could detect CAP over a broad LDR of 1.0 to 100 ng/mL and at a low level of LOD = 0.13 pg/mL. Xu et al. [160] designed a colorimetric assay for KNM detection in milk samples using AuNPs as the energy acceptor. Furthermore, three hairpin DNA probes were absorbed on the AuNPs, containing one aptamer hairpin DNA probe (for target identification) and two hairpin DNA probes (for signal amplification) (Fig. 8B). In this strategy, a series of hybridization chain reactions (HCR) take place after the addition of KNM, which causes aptamer to bind to the analyte. AuNPs assisted signal conversion by facilitating signal amplification via HCR. This method was capable of sensing KNM in a detection threshold of 0.68 μM and a LDR of 1 to 40 μM.

In another study, a rapid and label-free detection assay were reported for tobramycin (TOB) in milk samples using 13.0 nm diameter unmodified AuNPs with a low LOD = 23.3 nM and linearity of 40 to 200 nM [161]. This colorimetric aptasensor offered excellent recovery of natamycin (97.7–105.1%) with high sensitivity, stability, and specificity in the presence of principal interferences from milk samples including some analogous ANBs, several metal ions, and some proteins (Table 3). This remarkable sensitivity/selectivity was achieved due to the aggregation of AuNPs brought on by the salt and the particular interactions between the DNA-aptamer and tobramycin. Adding aptamer to AuNPs prevents aggregation through electrostatic repulsion due to the negative charges of DNA and AuNPs. Aggregation occurs with cationic particles. UV–Vis spectra detect dispersion, showing absorbance decrease at 520 nm and a new peak at 620 nm in aggregations. Reportedly, there was a significant hue shift from red to blue that was visible to the naked eye (Fig. 8A).

Using a simple and incredibly sensitive colorimetric sensor based on the interaction between lysine-functionalized and AuNPs, Zhang et al. [162] developed a novel assay for detecting gentamicin in milk. GEN's numerous amino and hydroxyl groups contribute to its strong ability to form hydrogen bonds. Thus, by adding GEN, the AuNP aggregation was promoted after the strong hydrogen binding between GEN and lysine (Fig. 8C). As a result, the color of AuNPs changed noticeably from red to blue, which was visible to the naked eye. The suggested technique displayed the LOD of gentamicin to be as low as 22.6 nM, with a LDR = 5 to 60 nM.

In order to quickly and colorimetrically identify STP and KNM, Saratale et al. [163] created a very sensitive colorimetric probe based on AgNPs using the SPR technique. CTC was coated on AgNPs (AgNPs-CTC) to act as the sensing element. AgNPs-CTC probe aggregation was regarded as a selective and sensitive ratiometric assay for the examined analytes. This sensing system relies on the cationic properties of aminoglycoside ANBs (STP and KNM) along with negatively charged AgNPs. Opposite charges between AgNPs and tested ANBs resulted in SPR band shift and NPs aggregation in solutions. AgNPs-CTC undergo a bathochromic shift (400 to 540 nm) and color change (yellow to red) owing to analyte-induced close contact. ANB amine groups linked AgNPs, promoting electrostatic interactions and forming distinct AgNP aggregates. This colorimetric probe was utilized to identify STP and KNM, with LOD =2000 and 120 pM, respectively.

Different sensing units, like aptamers or antibosies, peptides, and other biomolecules, can immobilize on the surface of AuNPs and AgNPs and stabilize the NPs, which can regulate salt-or target analyte-induced aggregation by mitigating surface plasmon resonance coupling between adjacent particles [149]. These NPs exhibit unique optical characteristics that cause a color shift from red to purple-blue (AuNPs) and yellow to orange/red (AgNPs) depending on their proximity, easily detectable visually and measurable using a spectroscopy analysis. For high-throughput screenings, using functionalized AgNPs instead of AuNPs is more cost-effective becasue of gold's affordability constraints.

In the colorimetric analysis of ANB, the MNPs have indicated promising potential as NMs, since MNPs can quickly collapse under a magnetic field through magnetic separation. Nevertheless, MNPs easily aggregate into clusters because the attractive van der Waals forces limit their catalytic properties. Thus, reducing the chance of aggregation through functionalization or surface modification of MNPs is a key factor in further improving their catalytic activity [164]. In one study, an innovative colorimetric aptasensor based on Fe3O4@Cu NPs and “sandwich” oligonucleotide hybridization, which exhibits peroxidase-like enzymatic activity for ultra-sensitive detection of TTC residue in spiked milk samples, was proposed by Wang et al. [165]. In the lack of TTC from the detection system, this “sandwich” oligonucleotide hybridization probe was designed in 96-wells plates by DNA hybridization strategy based on the following segments: i) cDNA1; a short amino-rectified complementary, a part of the TTC aptamer sequence, ii) cDNA2; a long complementary to 5′ terminal TTC aptamer sequence, and iii) anti TTC aptamers. This unique oligonucleotide probe had a lot of Fe3O4@Cu nano-enzymes placed on itself, which increased its peroxidase-like activity.

In a recenr study, a rapid and label-free colorimetric detection assay were reported for norfloxacin (NFX) in milk samples using Fe3O4 magnetic MIP nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MMIP NPs) with a low LOD = 9 ng/mL and linearity of 10–300 ng/mL [166]. The Fe3O4 MMIP NPs indicated good peroxidase-like catalytic properties to TMB and capacity to selectively capture NFX.TMB was choosed as the chromogenic agent for NPs and the system was fabricated based on the Fe3O4 MMIP NPs-H2O2-TMB reaction system. In this respect, the Fe3O4 MMIP NPs indicated a considerable affinity and maximal reaction speed toward TMB and H2O2. This can be ascribed to their small particle sizes, extensive selective surface areas, and abundant surface charges, facilitating the adsorption of positively charged TMB and showing a potent attraction to TMB. The suggested technique displayed the LOD of gentamicin to be as low as 22.6 nM, with a LDR = 5 to 60 nM.

Generally, MNPs have garnered significant attention in colorimetric detection techniques due to their unique ability to be swiftly separated under a magnetic field, facilitating easy handling and separation [164]. Fe3O4, a type of metal oxides-based MNPs, have been extensively researched as they were the first peroxidase mimic to be reported, thanks to their outstanding magnetic attributes. Fe3O4 have a tendency to aggregate into clusters owing to van der Waals attractive forces, consequently limiting their catalytic effectiveness. Thus, their surface modification or functionalization can play an important role in reducing aggregation and enhancing the catalytic performance of these NPs [164].

SPR detection techniques have ignited a surge of unprecedented interest as a timely and label-free surface strategy for trace substance molecular recognition. It is a physical phenomenon related to the loss of radiant light energy in the metal layer and the reduction of light intensity. This electromagnetic surface wave can be utilized for ANB analysis using the angle of the reflected SPR waves. Indeed, after the functionalization of the metal surface with a ligand, the attachment of the target analyte causes a local increase in refractive index (RI). This can be directly determined by SPR red-shift [53,167,168]. However, it should be noted that the RI changes on the surface are often negligible due to small mass, which can be difficult to identify ANBr [169]. Thus, the use of different NMs and consideration of various signal amplification strategies is a significant research focus of SPR detection methods for ANB recognition (Table 3).

In a different study, Altintas [170] presented a nano-MIP sensor based on surface plasmon resonance (SPR) for ultrahigh sensitivity, quick and effective detection of vancomycin (VAN). In this experiment, VAN was combined with AuNPs and then the competitive analysis between VAN and AuNPs-VAN was examined by blending samples with free VAN and VAN conjugated with AuNPs in skimmed milk. In this regard, using an optical sensor, the synthetic receptors could detect VAN with good sensitivity and selectivity in the shortest assay time (4 min). The results demonstrated a LDR from 10 to 1000 ng, and a LOD = 17.7 ng/mL. Notably, it has been reported that the MIPs are particularly promising for trace substance detection since the RI of MIPs significantly changes, when the target molecule is present in solution. As such, the use of NPs-based MIPs for the development of SPR detection techniques has recently indicated promising potential for ANBrs detection in actual complex samples [171].

In one such study, Bakhshpour et al. [172] proposed a MIP-based SPR detection technique for the sensitive/selective identification of PNC-G from actual samples based on the decoration of poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-N-methacroyl-(L)-cysteine methyl ester)-N-methacryloyl-L-phenylalanine methyl ester using AgNPs. Further, the non-imprinted sensors were fabricated without the inclusion of the PNC-G template for assessing the imprinting effect using the same polymerization recipe. After characterization of NpMIPs SPR sensor, kinetic behavior, and SPR signal response, the PNC-G amount present in the milk sample was determined with a LDR of 0.01–10 ng/mL. Similarly, the PNC-G imprinted SPR sensor was fabricated based on the integration of AuNPs into the poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-N-methacroyl-(L)-cysteine methyl ester structure [173]. First, similar to the previous study, NpMIPs AuNPs-based SPR sensors were prepared without the addition of the PNC-G template molecule for evaluating the imprinting efficiency. Then, the effect of AuNPs to improve the SPR signal response was evaluated by preparation of a nanosensor without the addition of AuNPs. Finally, after spiking PNC − G into the milk sample, the amount of PNC − G was successfully determined with linearity of 0.01 to 0.25 ppb (LOD = 0.00017 ppb). Cross-reactivity analysis also indicated that the MIP-AuNPs SPR sensor was 9.87 and 16.78 times more selective for the target PNC − G molecule than AMX and AMP, respectively.

Gao et al. [167] announced the development of a new SPR sensor based on magnetic-MIP@Fe3O4NPs for the very sensitive detection of TTC. The surface printing method was used to produce MMIP NPs. Next, MIP was functionalized by Fe3O4 NPs containing carboxyl groups and used as a carrier. In this investigation, MMIPs served as a label to strengthen the SPR signal and as a probe to detect TTC. This MIP-based SPR biosensor achieved an ultralow LOD (1.0 pg/mL) in the linear working range of 5.0–100 pg/mL.

Generally, quantity recognition of ANBrs from complex food and liquid matrices is a very challenging and time-consuming process because of the large quantity of ANBs with similar structures and complex matrices. Many attempts have recently been carried out to address this issue. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a powerful strategy for the identification of ANBr in different food matrices (Table 3). SERS improves the Raman scattering signal of ANBs through SERS-active platforms, including toughed noble metal colloids or NMNPs, nanospheres, etc. SERS is uninfluenced by water molecules, and can offer special fingerprint spectrograms of target analytes; thus, it provides a proper strategy for the simultaneous identification of ANBs from complex food and liquid samples [53,174]. In one such study, Hongji Li’s group [175] developed an AgNP-based SERS membrane using the SERS detection process and membrane separation for the ultra-sensitive recognition of enrofloxacin (ENR) in real milk samples. Reportedly, the SERS membrane was prepared via filtration of AgNPs on its surface and assessing the level of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and AgNPs to the sensitivity [175]. Meanwhile, the analytes were also effectively enriched to enhance the sensitivity using membrane separation. Besides, the addition of AgNPs efficiently enhanced the hydrophilicity of the SERS membrane to improve the recognition efficiency in the aqueous samples. Under the optimized experimental parameters, the proposed SERS membrane can recognize ENR in the range of 1.0 nML to 200 nM.

Jiang et al. [176] investigated a simple and accurate KNM detection method employing aptamer-based SERS sensor based on 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) modified with Au@Ag core-shell NPs. By using seed-mediated growth, the Au@AgNPs with uniform morphologies and diameters of 32.2–3.4 nm was synthesized. Then, the Raman reporter 4-MBA was applied to this NC to create a substrate with a high SERS activity. Due to NMNPs' shared advantages of high stability and enhanced surface electrical activity, this aptasensing method demonstrated good sensitivity, specificity, and stability with a low LOD = 142 pg/mL throughout a LDR of 6.67 × 10−10 to 2 × 10−7 g/mL. Xie and coworkers [177] prepared solid-phase microextraction (SPME) AgNP-based SERS platform for the rapid, facile, and ultra-sensitive detection of degraded-ANB (MMT; 2-mercapto-5-methyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole) in milk samples. In this study, the AgNP-decorated TiO2 nanotubes were successfully utilized as an integrated SPME@SERS platform for the extraction and recognition of the degraded MMT compounds in milk samples [178]. Based on the log–log plot of SERS intensity, the developed SERS substrate can quantitatively analyze MMT with an excellent LDR of 0.5–1000 μM (R2 ∼ 0.99) and low LOD = 0.11 μM with a rapid pre-treatment and proper reproducibility.

Fan et al. [178] developed a SERS sensing mechanism for the fast and facil recognition of TTC and PNC in milk samples. The proposed system can qualitatively determine ANBs by their SERS peaks at 1332 and 1079 cm−1. Then, the TTC and PNC were quantitively characterized at 1079 and 1332 cm−1, respectively. After optimization, the LOD of TTC and PNC could be as low as 0.015 ng/mL and 0.010 ng/mL, respectively, with satisfactory recoveries (88.8 to 111.3%). Lately, Guoqing Chen's group [179] created an AgNP-based SERS system for tracking NFX residue in milk sample. The proposed SERS sensing mechanism used β-cyclodextrin and AgNPs as a detection substrate, which selectively captured the target NFX through a “host–guest” inclusion process [179] .Under the optimized conditions, LOD of NFX as low as 3.214 pM and 5.327 nM in standard solution and spiked milk was achieved, respectively.

Changing people's lifestyles and nutrition habitus has intensified the request for nourishing and healthy food. Providing safe and sufficient food in these vast dimensions requires developing reliable health-controlling mechanisms with economic justification. Although production of ANBs has been a practical solution for healthy food, the development of bacterial immunity and ANBr has become a daily challenge for food safety researchers. Novel detection methods with a futuristic viewpoint has the potential to solve this problem. Innovation in bio−/nano-sensors has been remarkably grown in the last few years. NMs' bonding capabilities to ANBr and biological polymers have presented fast and reliable instruments for quantifying and qualifying these food hazards. In this research, we have provided a comprehensive overview of NMs-based sensors and their application for the dairy industry.
